meta.method: rubric + AI-assisted (Claude), grounded in deterministic metrics; includes field missing-topics and emerging-trends analysis

b-a-in-computer-science-226.name: B.A. in Computer Science

dimensions.name: Curriculum coherence & progression
dimensions.justification: The 8-term plan follows a clean CS spine (CS 100 -> CS 113 -> CS 114 -> CS 241/CS 280 -> CS 288/CS 331/CS 332 -> CS 350/CS 356 -> CS 435/CS 490 -> CS 491 Senior Project) with prerequisite depths rising smoothly from 0 to 6 and no abrupt difficulty jumps. As a B.A. it deliberately trades depth for breadth, so the upper-level path leans on repeated generic placeholders ('CS Elective 300 or above', 'CS/IS/IT Elective 200 or above') and omits the theory-II and data-science courses that the B.S. requires.
dimensions.suggestion: Replace the repeated generic upper-level elective placeholders with 2-3 named recommended tracks (e.g., applied AI, security, systems) so B.A. students see coherent pathways instead of open slots.

dimensions.name: Prerequisite structure health
dimensions.justification: The prerequisite graph is well-shaped with a deepest chain of 6 (CS 100 -> 113 -> 114 -> 241 -> CS 435 -> CS 490 -> CS 491), reasonable for a four-year CS degree, and the core shows no over- or under-constraining. All 5 dangling references (missingCourseCount 5: GER 200, GER 300, PHYS 111A, YWCC 207, YWCC 307) are GER placeholders, a lab, and one-credit seminars rather than genuine CS prerequisite breaks.
dimensions.suggestion: Add catalog entries or explicit code mappings for YWCC 207, YWCC 307 and PHYS 111A, and resolve the GER 200/GER 300 placeholders so no referenced code dangles.

dimensions.name: Learning outcomes & objectives
dimensions.justification: No program-level learning outcomes or educational objectives appear anywhere in the provided data; only the term-by-term plan of study and per-course descriptions are present. For a catalog page this absence is a real finding, and it is especially costly for a B.A. whose value proposition (breadth plus a second field) needs an explicit statement.
dimensions.suggestion: Publish an explicit set of program learning outcomes describing what a B.A. graduate can design, analyze, and communicate, and how the breadth electives are meant to be used.

dimensions.name: Credit distribution & balance
dimensions.justification: The plan reconciles exactly: summedTermCredits 120 equals statedTotalCredits 120 with creditReconcileDelta 0.0, and terms are evenly loaded at 14-16 credits. The mix transparently spans a CS core, a lighter math/science block (MATH 111/112/333, no required linear algebra), GER, and multiple free electives appropriate to a B.A.
dimensions.suggestion: Add a short credit-summary breakdown (major core vs. GER vs. free/CS electives) so the B.A.'s heavier elective share is stated explicitly rather than only implied by the term plan.

dimensions.name: Presentation & clarity
dimensions.justification: Terms are clearly labeled by year and semester and required courses are legible, but the plan carries parsing artifacts that hurt clarity: a stray cross-program line ('228 B.S. in Computer Science') appears mid-plan in Year 4, a null-code FYS seminar row shows 0 credits, and the COM 312 requirement is split across an 'or COM 313 or Technical Writing' fragment row.
dimensions.suggestion: Clean the plan-of-study rendering so alternative courses ('COM 312 or COM 313') are single legible entries and remove the cross-program artifact line ('228 B.S. in Computer Science').

dimensions.name: Industry & currency relevance
dimensions.justification: The required core covers durable, employable fundamentals (databases CS 331, operating systems CS 332, networks CS 356, systems CS 350, Linux tooling CS 288, advanced algorithms CS 435, software engineering CS 490, team Senior Project CS 491), but unlike the B.S. this B.A. requires no data science (CS 301), no cybersecurity (CS 351), and no AI/ML, leaving every modern high-demand area to unspecified electives; CS 288 also still foregrounds a dated PHP/MySQL stack.
dimensions.suggestion: Require at least one modern applied course (intro AI/ML, data science, or security) in the B.A. core and refresh CS 288's stack emphasis away from PHP toward current cloud/data tooling.

descriptionQuality.justification: Every sampled course has a present, specific description with real technical substance (e.g., CS 241 on algorithm design/proof and complexity, CS 356 on layered architectures and routing, CS 435 on graphs and hash tables); only the MATH 111/112 entries are terse topic lists rather than full descriptions.

prerequisiteClarity.justification: Prerequisites are stated with explicit grade conditions and sensible OR-alternatives (e.g., CS 280 'CS 114 or CS 116 or IT 114 or equivalent with a grade C or better'), and the core chain is internally consistent. Clarity is dinged only by inconsistent phrasing ('grade C or better' vs 'grade of C or higher') and prerequisites that point at dangling GER placeholders.

missingTopics.rationale: A 2026 CS degree should give every graduate baseline ML exposure, but this B.A. requires none (CS 301 Introduction to Data Science is required only in the B.S.); add a required or clearly recommended intro-AI/ML course.

The B.A. omits CS 351 Introduction to Cybersecurity that the B.S. requires, leaving graduates without any guaranteed secure-coding or threat-modeling grounding; make CS 351 a required or default elective.

CS 332 (OS) and CS 356 (networks) cover single-machine and network fundamentals but nothing on cloud deployment, containers, or distributed computing that dominate current practice; add a cloud/distributed-systems elective.

The B.A. requires only MATH 333, omitting the MATH 337 Linear Algebra the B.S. requires, which is foundational for graphics, ML, and data work; recommend or require linear algebra for students eyeing those tracks.

CS 490 is the sole SWE course and CS 288 still centers PHP/MySQL, so version control, testing/CI-CD, and API design are underweighted; modernize CS 288 or add a practical software-engineering elective.

emergingTrends.rationale: Employers now expect graduates to build with and reason about LLM tooling; CS 490 and the CS 491 Senior Project are natural places to require an AI-assisted development component.
Data and model pipelines are among the fastest-growing CS roles, yet this B.A. has no required data course; a data-engineering elective would let breadth-oriented B.A. students target this market.
Secure defaults and privacy-by-design are becoming baseline professional expectations, reinforcing the case for embedding CS 351 content rather than leaving security optional.
The existing IS 350 (Computers, Society and Ethics) is a strong hook to extend into algorithmic accountability and AI governance, differentiating the B.A.'s breadth angle.
Industry has shifted to containerized, serverless deployment; positioning an elective here would keep the B.A. current without adding heavy theory load.

topSuggestions: Publish explicit program-level learning outcomes for the B.A., which are entirely absent from the catalog data.
Add at least one required modern applied course (intro AI/ML, data science, or cybersecurity) so the B.A. does not leave every high-demand area to unspecified electives.
Replace the repeated generic elective placeholders with named recommended tracks (applied AI, security, systems) to give the upper-level path coherence.
Modernize CS 288's dated PHP/MySQL emphasis toward current cloud and data tooling.
Clean plan-of-study parsing artifacts (the stray '228 B.S. in Computer Science' line and split 'or COM 313' rows) and resolve the 5 dangling references (YWCC 207/307, PHYS 111A, GER 200/300).

b-a-in-information-systems-249.name: B.A. in Information Systems

dimensions.name: Curriculum coherence & progression
dimensions.justification: The B.A. keeps the same well-ordered IS spine as its B.S. siblings (CS 100 -> IS 117 -> IS 218 -> IS 331; IS 265 -> IS 344 -> IS 465; IS 247/IS 375 UX; IS 390 requirements analysis; IS 455 IS management) converging on an IS 491/IT 491 capstone, with a lighter quantitative load (MATH 138, MATH 105) befitting a liberal-arts degree. Progression is logical, but the plan is very elective-driven -- 'IS Career Track Electives 1-3', 'New Media or Business Specialization Electives 1-5', and multiple general electives -- so the required technical arc is comparatively thin and coherence depends on students choosing a coherent track.
dimensions.suggestion: Define named, pre-packaged tracks (e.g., New Media vs. Business) with a fixed course sequence rather than a menu of interchangeable 'Specialization Elective' slots, so each path reads as a coherent progression.

dimensions.name: Prerequisite structure health
dimensions.justification: The prerequisite graph mirrors the department's healthy IS structure: deepestPrereqChain is 7 (IS 465 Advanced IS as terminal), with foundational courses shallow and no over-constraining. missingCourseCount is 3 and all are peripheral -- GER 200 is a general-education placeholder and YWCC 207/YWCC 307 are the one-credit professional-development seminars present in the plan but absent from the catalog.
dimensions.suggestion: Resolve the three dangling references by cataloguing YWCC 207/YWCC 307 and re-tagging GER 200 as a requirement category.

dimensions.name: Learning outcomes & objectives
dimensions.justification: No program-level learning outcomes, objectives, or overview appear in the data. Because the B.A. differentiates itself from the B.S. mainly through breadth and track flexibility, the absence of an explicit outcomes statement makes it especially hard for a student to understand what distinguishes this degree.
dimensions.suggestion: Publish learning outcomes that articulate the B.A.'s emphasis (IS analysis/design plus a humanities/new-media or business specialization) and a short overview clarifying how it differs from the B.S.

dimensions.name: Credit distribution & balance
dimensions.justification: The plan reconciles cleanly (statedTotalCredits 120, summedTermCredits 120, creditReconcileDelta 0.0), which is a real strength. Transparency, however, is undercut by the sheer number of unspecified slots -- five 'Specialization Electives', three 'IS Career Track Electives', and several general electives -- meaning a large share of the 120 credits is not visibly defined, which is characteristic of a B.A. but reduces at-a-glance clarity.
dimensions.suggestion: Publish a compact credit-distribution summary (gen-ed vs. IS core vs. track electives vs. free electives) and cap/label the elective menus so the balance between required and open credit is transparent.

dimensions.name: Presentation & clarity
dimensions.justification: The structure is intact and terms are labeled, but footnote superscripts bleed heavily into titles ('General Elective 1 3,7', 'IS Career Track Elective 1 4', 'New Media or Business Specialization Elective 1 5', 'MATH 138 | General Calculus I 1'), GER rows are split with null credits, and duplicated 'Select one of the following:' lines and 'or COM 313 or Technical Writing' options render as unresolved rows -- collectively noisy even though nothing is truncated.
dimensions.suggestion: Strip the footnote reference numbers out of the title text (render them as footnotes) and collapse the 'Select one of the following' / 'or ...' groupings into single readable choice lines.

dimensions.name: Industry & currency relevance
dimensions.justification: The IS core is current -- database design (IS 331), UX (IS 247/IS 375), requirements analysis (IS 390), IS management (IS 455/IS 465), networking (IT 120) -- and the career-track flexibility is a genuine plus. But as a B.A. the technical depth is modest: analytics is limited to what IS 465 touches, there is no security/governance course, no cloud/SaaS exposure, and the only programming is web, so graduates may need to supplement with data or security skills employers now expect.
dimensions.suggestion: Steer at least one required 'IS Career Track Elective' toward a modern data-analytics or information-security course so every B.A. graduate leaves with one concrete, in-demand technical competency beyond web development.

descriptionQuality.justification: All 25 sampled courses carry present, specific descriptions with no missing/placeholder entries (e.g., IS 218 web-based information systems, IS 331 databases for decision-making, IS 465 data analysis/statistics from an IS perspective, IS 350 computing ethics). Descriptions are informative rather than thin, though some registrar titles are abbreviated.

prerequisiteClarity.justification: Prerequisites are stated with explicit grade gates and coherent alternatives (IS 390: 'CS 103, CS 113, CS 115, IS 218 or IT 202'; IS 455: '(IS 265 or MIS 245) and IS 390'; COM 312/313 with ENGL 102 + GER conditions), and intro courses correctly show none. Minor issues -- GER-set placeholders embedded in IS 350's prerequisite and slight phrasing variance -- keep it from a 5.

missingTopics.rationale: Analytics is confined to IS 465; even a B.A. IS graduate is expected to query and visualize data, so a hands-on analytics course should anchor at least one career track.
No security or data-governance course exists despite IS 350 raising ethics/privacy conceptually; compliance and infosec literacy are baseline for IS roles.
The technical stack is web + networking (IT 120) with no cloud exposure, yet modern IS work is overwhelmingly delivered on cloud/SaaS ecosystems.
IE 492 is generic management; a dedicated Agile/PM course would connect IS 390 requirements work to how IS projects are actually run.
IS 455 treats processes conceptually; hands-on ERP or workflow-automation experience would make the business specialization concrete.

emergingTrends.trend: Generative AI and low-code application development
emergingTrends.rationale: IS professionals increasingly assemble solutions with AI copilots and low-code tools; a B.A. track could adopt this quickly to add applied value on top of IS 218/IS 344.
Configuration and integration of SaaS platforms is now core IS work and a natural modernization of IS 344/IS 455.

emergingTrends.trend: Self-service business intelligence and data storytelling
emergingTrends.rationale: Data-driven decision-making with self-service BI fits the B.A.'s communication strengths (COM 312/313) and would differentiate its graduates.
Organizations are automating processes IS 455 studies; RPA/process-mining exposure would make the business track current.
Extending IS 350 ethics into concrete governance/compliance aligns with a fast-growing, communication-heavy IS career path suited to a B.A.

topSuggestions: Publish program-level learning outcomes and an overview that clarifies how the B.A. differs from the B.S. in Information Systems.
Convert the loose 'Specialization Elective' menus into named, fixed tracks (New Media / Business) so each path is a coherent sequence.
Guarantee one modern technical competency per graduate by steering a career-track elective into applied analytics or information security.
Add cloud/SaaS and Agile-project-management options to close the most visible currency gaps.
Clean presentation: remove footnote numbers from titles and collapse split GER and 'or ...' rows into single lines.

b-s-in-business-and-information-systems-.name: B.S. in Business and Information Systems

dimensions.name: Curriculum coherence & progression
dimensions.justification: The eight-term plan interleaves a coherent IS spine (CS 100 -> IS 117 -> IS 218 -> IS 331 database; IS 265 -> IS 344 -> IS 465 Advanced IS; IS 247/IS 375 UX; IS 390 requirements analysis; IS 455 IS management & business processes) with a well-sequenced business core (ECON 201, ACCT 117, FIN 218, FIN 315, MGMT 216 Business Data Analytics, MRKT 330, MGMT 391, HRM 301). Term loads are steady at 12-16 credits and culminate in an IS 491/IT 491 capstone, so difficulty ramps smoothly. The main weakness is that the two disciplines run largely in parallel with only IS 344/IS 455/MGMT 216 explicitly bridging them.
dimensions.suggestion: Add an explicit integrative course or capstone theme that forces the business and IS threads together (e.g., an analytics-for-business-decisions project spanning IS 465 and MGMT 216) so the two spines converge rather than run side by side.

dimensions.name: Prerequisite structure health
dimensions.justification: The prerequisite graph is sensible: deepestPrereqChain is 7 (IS 465 Advanced IS, a terminal course gated on a statistical GER plus IS 265/IS 344 and IS 331), which is appropriate depth for a senior course and not over-constraining, while foundational courses sit at shallow depths. missingCourseCount is 4 and every dangling reference is peripheral -- GER 200 and GER 300 are general-education level placeholders, and YWCC 207/YWCC 307 are the one-credit professional-development seminars that appear in the plan but are not catalogued as course entries.
dimensions.suggestion: Resolve the four dangling references by linking YWCC 207/YWCC 307 to their catalog entries and re-tagging GER 200/GER 300 as requirement categories rather than course codes.

dimensions.name: Learning outcomes & objectives
dimensions.justification: The program data contains no program-level learning outcomes, educational objectives, or program overview. A prospective student can only infer the intended competencies by reading the course list, with no explicit statement of what a Business & IS graduate will know or be able to do -- a genuine gap for a catalog page.
dimensions.suggestion: Publish 5-8 program-level learning outcomes spanning IS analysis/design, database and web systems, business analytics, and professional communication, alongside a short program overview.

dimensions.name: Credit distribution & balance
dimensions.justification: Credit accounting reconciles cleanly: statedTotalCredits is 120, summedTermCredits is 120, and creditReconcileDelta is 0.0. The split is transparent and balanced across general education, a substantial business core, and the IS core, with roughly five general-elective slots providing flexibility. The only caveat is that the business load is heavy enough that quantitative/technical IS depth (programming, analytics) competes for limited room.
dimensions.suggestion: Publish a one-line credit summary (gen-ed vs. business core vs. IS core vs. electives) so the ~120-credit split is visible at a glance and the business/IS balance is explicit.

dimensions.name: Presentation & clarity
dimensions.justification: Terms are clearly labeled and most rows carry code, title, and credits, but several presentation defects remain: general-education rows are split and carry null credits (e.g., 'Science Literacy GER', 'History and Humanities GER 200 level'), alternative options render as separate null-code lines ('or ACCT 115 or Fundamentals of Financial Accounting', 'or COM 313 or Technical Writing'), and multiple titles are abbreviated ('Principles Of Fin Accountng', 'IS Mgmt & Business Processes', 'Computing & Effective Com').
dimensions.suggestion: Merge each split GER row and each 'or ...' alternative into a single readable line, and restore full course titles in place of the truncated registrar abbreviations.

dimensions.name: Industry & currency relevance
dimensions.justification: The program reflects real business-IS practice via database design (IS 331), e-commerce technology (IT 310), business data analytics (MGMT 216), UX (IS 247/IS 375), and IS management (IS 455/IS 465). However, for a contemporary Business & IS degree the analytics footprint is thin (essentially MGMT 216 plus the statistics touched in IS 465), there is no information-security/governance course, no enterprise-systems/ERP exposure, and the only programming is web development -- leaving a graduate short of the Python/SQL analytics fluency employers now expect.
dimensions.suggestion: Add a required data-analytics course using Python/SQL and a BI visualization tool (Tableau/Power BI), and a business information-security & governance elective, to align with current analyst and IS-management hiring.

descriptionQuality.justification: All 33 sampled courses carry present, specific descriptions with no missing or placeholder entries; excerpts convey real topical content (e.g., IS 218 'critical, hands-on introduction to the design of Web-based Information Systems', IS 331 databases for analysis/decision-making, IS 465 data analysis and statistics from an IS perspective). A few registrar titles are terse, but the descriptive text itself is informative rather than thin.

prerequisiteClarity.justification: Prerequisites are stated with explicit grade gates ('with a grade of C or better') and sensible boolean alternatives (IS 218: '(IS 117 or IT 202) and (CS 100, CS 113, or CS 115)'; IS 465 lists an explicit statistical-GER set). Intro courses correctly show no prerequisite. Minor blemishes -- GER-set placeholders inside prerequisite text (IS 350) and slight phrasing variance ('C or better' vs 'C or higher') -- keep it from a 5.

missingTopics.topic: Hands-on business/data analytics (Python, SQL, BI dashboards)
missingTopics.rationale: Analytics is limited to MGMT 216 and the statistics in IS 465; a modern Business & IS graduate needs applied Python/SQL and a dashboarding tool -- add a dedicated analytics course rather than relying on a single survey.

missingTopics.topic: Information security, risk & IT governance (GRC, SOX/GDPR)
missingTopics.rationale: No course addresses security or compliance despite IT 310 e-commerce and IS 455 business processes; business IS professionals are routinely responsible for data-protection and regulatory risk.

missingTopics.topic: Enterprise systems / ERP and process integration
missingTopics.rationale: IS 455 covers business processes conceptually but there is no hands-on ERP (SAP/Oracle) or process-integration experience that business-IS roles rely on.
IE 492 Engineering Management is generic; a dedicated Agile/PM course would match how IS projects (IS 390 requirements, IS 491 capstone) are actually delivered in industry.
Given the FIN/ACCT/MRKT reporting context, students would benefit from dimensional modeling and warehouse/BI pipeline concepts beyond the transactional focus of IS 331.

emergingTrends.trend: Generative AI for business productivity and analytics
emergingTrends.rationale: LLM copilots are reshaping accounting, marketing, and analyst workflows this program feeds; an 'AI for business' elective would build on MGMT 216 and IS 465.
Business systems increasingly run on Salesforce/ServiceNow/Power Platform; IS 344/IS 455 could be modernized to teach configuration and low-code app building.

emergingTrends.trend: Process mining and robotic process automation (RPA)
emergingTrends.rationale: IS 455 business-process content is a natural home for process mining and RPA, which are now central to operational-efficiency roles.
With FIN 218/FIN 315 and IT 310 e-commerce already present, the program is well positioned to add a FinTech elective covering digital payments and platform finance.

emergingTrends.trend: Data privacy and responsible data governance
emergingTrends.rationale: Building on IS 350 ethics, GDPR/CCPA-style governance is now a core expectation for anyone managing business information systems.

topSuggestions: Publish explicit program-level learning outcomes and a short overview; the data currently contains none.
Add a required applied-analytics course (Python/SQL + BI dashboards) to deepen the thin analytics footprint beyond MGMT 216.
Introduce an information-security/IT-governance course and an enterprise-systems/ERP experience to close the two largest industry gaps.
Add an Agile/project-management course to support IS 390 and the IS 491 capstone with real delivery practice.
Clean the plan-of-study presentation: merge split GER and 'or ...' rows and restore truncated course titles.

b-s-in-computer-science-228.name: B.S. in Computer Science

dimensions.name: Curriculum coherence & progression
dimensions.justification: The 8-term plan is the fuller flagship path: the CS spine (CS 100 -> 113 -> 114 -> 241 -> 280/288 -> 332/350/356 -> 435/490 -> 491) is complemented by theory II (CS 341), data science (CS 301), cybersecurity (CS 351), and linear algebra (MATH 337), with prerequisite depths rising smoothly from 0 to 6. Coherence is only slightly weakened by four undifferentiated 'CS Elective 300 or above' slots concentrated in the senior year.
dimensions.suggestion: Group the four senior-year CS electives into 1-2 declared concentrations (e.g., AI/data, security, systems) so the upper-level plan reads as intentional depth rather than open choice.

dimensions.name: Prerequisite structure health
dimensions.justification: The prerequisite graph is healthy with a deepest chain of 6 and good breadth (CS 341 depth 5, CS 351 depth 5, CS 301 depth 6), and the required sequence is internally consistent with no genuine CS breaks. All 6 dangling references (missingCourseCount 6: GER 200, GER 300, PHYS 111A, PHYS 121A, YWCC 207, YWCC 307) are GER placeholders, physics labs, and one-credit seminars.
dimensions.suggestion: Resolve the physics-lab (PHYS 111A/121A) and YWCC 207/307 catalog entries and the GER placeholders so the metrics show zero dangling references.

dimensions.name: Learning outcomes & objectives
dimensions.justification: No program-level learning outcomes or ABET-style educational objectives appear in the provided data despite this being the college's flagship accredited-track B.S.; only the plan of study and course descriptions are present. This absence is a real catalog finding.
dimensions.suggestion: Publish explicit program learning outcomes / student outcomes (design, analysis, communication, professional responsibility), which also supports program accreditation reporting.

dimensions.name: Credit distribution & balance
dimensions.justification: The plan reconciles exactly: summedTermCredits 120 equals statedTotalCredits 120 with creditReconcileDelta 0.0, and terms are evenly loaded at 14-16 credits. The distribution transparently balances a deep CS core, a math/science block (MATH 111/112/333/337, PHYS 111/121 with labs), GER, and upper-level CS electives.
dimensions.suggestion: Add an explicit credit-summary table (major core vs. math/science vs. GER vs. CS electives) to make the well-balanced split visible without reading all eight terms.

dimensions.name: Presentation & clarity
dimensions.justification: Terms are clearly labeled and the required courses are legible, but the plan contains parsing artifacts: a stray cross-program line ('230 B.S. in Computer Science and B.S. in Applied Physics') appears in Year 4, the COM 312 requirement is split across an 'or COM 313 or Technical Writing' fragment, and a null-code FYS seminar row shows 0 credits.
dimensions.suggestion: Render alternative-course requirements ('COM 312 or COM 313') as single entries and remove the cross-program artifact line so the B.S. plan is self-contained.

dimensions.name: Industry & currency relevance
dimensions.justification: The required core reflects current practice well: it mandates data science (CS 301), cybersecurity (CS 351), databases (CS 331), networks (CS 356), systems (CS 350), advanced algorithms (CS 435), software engineering (CS 490), and a team Senior Project (CS 491). The main currency gaps are the lack of a required dedicated AI/ML course beyond CS 301 and no cloud/distributed-systems requirement, plus CS 288's dated PHP/MySQL framing.
dimensions.suggestion: Add a required or default intro-AI/ML course and a cloud/distributed-systems elective, and refresh CS 288's stack toward current cloud-native tooling.

descriptionQuality.justification: Sampled descriptions are present and specific with genuine technical content (CS 341 on automata/computability/complexity, CS 351 on cybersecurity fundamentals, CS 301 on data-science models and hands-on skills); only the MATH entries (111/112/337) are terse topic lists.

prerequisiteClarity.justification: Prerequisites are explicit with consistent grade conditions and sensible compound logic (e.g., CS 341 '(CS 241 or MATH 226) and CS 280 with a grade C or better', CS 351 'CS 241 and CS 356'), and the required chain is internally consistent. Only minor phrasing inconsistency and dangling GER references detract.

missingTopics.topic: Dedicated artificial intelligence / machine learning course
missingTopics.rationale: CS 301 introduces data-science models but the B.S. lacks a required AI/ML course covering supervised/unsupervised learning and neural networks in depth; add a required CS 4xx Machine Learning to match peer programs.

CS 332 (OS) and CS 356 (networks) stop at the single node and LAN; there is no coverage of containers, orchestration, or distributed consensus that define modern backends, so a distributed-systems/cloud course is warranted.
Concurrency is only touched inside CS 332; with multicore and GPU hardware ubiquitous, a dedicated parallel-programming course would strengthen the systems track.

missingTopics.topic: Software engineering breadth (testing, DevOps, version control)
missingTopics.rationale: CS 490 is the single SWE course and CS 288 centers PHP/MySQL, so CI/CD, automated testing, and API design are underweighted relative to industry hiring expectations.
The plan has no interaction-design content, a gap for graduates building user-facing products; a HCI elective would round out the largely back-end and theory core.

emergingTrends.rationale: Building, evaluating, and deploying LLM-based systems is now a core industry skill; the CS 491 Senior Project and CS 490 are ideal vehicles to require applied generative-AI work.
CS 301 gives a data-science foundation that could extend into production ML pipelines, one of the fastest-growing CS job categories.
Industry deployment has moved to containerized, serverless, and edge patterns that the current systems sequence does not address, motivating a cloud requirement.
With CS 351 already required, the B.S. is well positioned to lead on secure-by-default development and on defending ML systems against adversarial attacks.
With a strong theory core (CS 241/CS 341) and math base, a quantum-computing elective would future-proof the program's theoretical distinctiveness.

topSuggestions: Publish explicit program learning outcomes / student outcomes, which are absent and are also needed for accreditation reporting.
Add a required dedicated AI/ML course and a cloud/distributed-systems requirement to close the two biggest currency gaps in an otherwise strong core.
Organize the four senior-year 'CS Elective 300 or above' slots into declared concentrations (AI/data, security, systems) for intentional depth.
Modernize CS 288's PHP/MySQL emphasis toward current cloud-native and data tooling.
Clean plan-of-study artifacts (the stray '230 ...' cross-program line and split 'or COM 313' rows) and resolve the 6 dangling references (PHYS 111A/121A, YWCC 207/307, GER 200/300).

b-s-in-computer-science-and-b-s-in-appli.name: B.S. in Computer Science and B.S. in Applied Physics

dimensions.name: Curriculum coherence & progression
dimensions.justification: The five-year plan builds logically along three well-ordered spines: CS (CS 100 -> 113 -> 114 -> 241/280 -> 341/350/435 -> 490/491), physics (PHYS 111 -> 121 -> 234 -> 335 -> 430/432 -> 442/485), and math (MATH 111 -> 112 -> 211 -> 222/335), with a deepest prereq chain of 7 confirming genuine term-over-term progression. Integration points such as CS 301 Data Science, PHYS 485 Computer Modeling, and OPSE 310 Virtual Instrumentation sensibly tie the two disciplines together, though the first year is heavily front-loaded at 18 credits.
dimensions.suggestion: Add a curriculum map showing how the physics track feeds the computing courses (e.g., PHYS 485 modeling drawing on CS 301/CS 435) so the dual-degree rationale is explicit rather than implied by co-scheduling.

dimensions.name: Prerequisite structure health
dimensions.justification: The prerequisite graph is sensible with a deepest chain of 7, reasonable for a rigorous 5-year dual degree, and grade conditions are clean throughout. The 7 dangling references (missingCourseCount 7: CHEM 125A, CHEM 126A, PHYS 111A, PHYS 121A, PHYS 231A, GER 200, GER 300) are all lab co-requisites or GER category placeholders rather than broken CS/physics chains.
dimensions.suggestion: Register the lab co-requisites (CHEM 125A/126A, PHYS 111A/121A/231A) and the GER 200/300 category codes so these dangling references resolve and missingCourseCount drops toward zero.

dimensions.name: Learning outcomes & objectives
dimensions.justification: No program-level learning outcomes or educational objectives appear anywhere in the provided data; only a plan of study and course list are present. This is a real catalog finding, and it matters more here because a dual degree needs an explicit statement of the integrated computing-plus-physics competency it produces.
dimensions.suggestion: Publish 5-8 program learning outcomes covering computing foundations, applied-physics modeling, and integrated computational problem-solving directly on the catalog page.

dimensions.name: Credit distribution & balance
dimensions.justification: The plan reconciles exactly (creditReconcileDelta 0.0; summedTermCredits 134 = statedTotalCredits 134), and the distribution spans a heavy STEM core (CS, physics, math, chemistry) with writing, ethics (IS 350), and GER woven in. Load is uneven, however: the first year is 18 credits while the two final terms drop to 12, and 134 credits is light for a true BS/BS pairing, worth confirming.
dimensions.suggestion: Level the term loads (redistribute from the 18-credit first year) and annotate the gen-ed vs. major-core vs. elective credit split so the balance is transparent.

dimensions.name: Presentation & clarity
dimensions.justification: Terms are clearly labeled with per-term credits shown, but the page carries PDF-extraction artifacts: 'or MATH 328 or Mathematical Methods...' and 'or R750 404 or Quantum Mechanics' alternatives appear as separate credit-less rows, and a stray '232 B.S. in Computer Science and B.S. in Mathematical Sciences' line bleeds in from an adjacent program.
dimensions.suggestion: Clean the plan-of-study parse so 'or ...' alternatives fold into their parent course and the stray '232 ...' cross-program fragment is removed.

dimensions.name: Industry & currency relevance
dimensions.justification: The course set is strong for scientific-computing careers: CS 301 (data science), CS 351 (cybersecurity), CS 356 (networks), CS 435 (algorithms), CS 490 (software engineering) on the computing side, and PHYS 442 Quantum Mechanics, PHYS 485 Computer Modeling of Applied Physics Problems, and OPSE 310 Virtual Instrumentation on the physics side. The main gaps are a dedicated ML/AI course and any explicit high-performance/parallel computing for large-scale simulation.
dimensions.suggestion: Add a machine-learning-for-science elective and an HPC/GPU computing option so graduates can apply modern compute to the physical-modeling work PHYS 485 introduces.

descriptionQuality.justification: Sampled descriptions are present, specific, and informative, naming concrete topics (CS 341 on automata/computability/complexity, CS 435 on balanced trees/hashing/graph algorithms, MATH 335 on Gauss/Green/Stokes theorems, PHYS 485 on computer modeling). None read as thin placeholders; only excerpts are truncated.

prerequisiteClarity.justification: Prerequisites are stated unambiguously with consistent grade conditions ('with a grade C or better') and sensible alternatives (e.g., CS 280 'CS 114 or CS 116 or IT 114'), and intro courses correctly show none. Clarity is only modestly reduced by references to lab co-reqs and GER categories that are not themselves catalogued.

missingTopics.rationale: Computational physics depends on numerical analysis, yet MATH 328 (Mathematical Methods) is only an 'or' option and no dedicated numerical-methods course is required; make a numerical-methods course a required bridge feeding PHYS 485.
PHYS 485 Computer Modeling and OPSE 310 imply large simulations but the plan has no GPU/parallel/HPC course; add one so students can run realistic physics workloads.

missingTopics.topic: Machine learning for the physical sciences
missingTopics.rationale: CS 301 introduces data science but there is no ML course applied to scientific data or surrogate modeling, now central to computational physics; add a scientific-ML elective.
Neither the CS nor physics track includes visualization or large scientific-dataset analysis, a core skill for experimental and simulation work; add a data-visualization module or course.
With PHYS 442 Quantum Mechanics plus a strong CS theory core (CS 241/CS 341), the program is unusually positioned to offer quantum computing but currently does not; add a quantum-computing elective.

emergingTrends.rationale: The rare combination of required quantum mechanics (PHYS 442) and CS theory makes this dual degree ideally placed to lead on quantum algorithms as the field industrializes.
Physics-informed neural networks and ML surrogates are transforming simulation; CS 301 plus PHYS 485 are natural anchors for a scientific-ML pathway.
Modern applied-physics modeling runs on accelerators and HPC clusters, so an HPC skill set would directly extend PHYS 485's computer-modeling work.
Coupling OPSE 310 instrumentation with simulation points toward digital-twin engineering, a growing area in aerospace, energy, and manufacturing.
Automated, ML-guided experimentation is reshaping physics labs; the program's instrumentation and computing base could position graduates for this niche.

topSuggestions: Publish explicit program learning outcomes for the integrated CS-plus-physics competency (currently none are present).
Require a numerical-methods/scientific-computing course and add an HPC/parallel-computing option to properly support PHYS 485 computer modeling.
Add a scientific-machine-learning elective and, leveraging PHYS 442, a quantum-computing elective to occupy the program's distinctive niche.
Level the uneven term loads (18-credit first year vs. 12-credit final terms) and annotate the gen-ed/major-core/elective credit split.
Clean the plan-of-study artifacts (fold 'or MATH 328' / 'or R750 404' alternatives into their parent rows, remove the stray '232 ...' line) and register the 7 dangling lab/GER references.

b-s-in-computer-science-and-b-s-in-mathe.name: B.S. in Computer Science and B.S. in Mathematical Sciences, Applied Mathematics

dimensions.name: Curriculum coherence & progression
dimensions.justification: Within the visible terms the CS spine (CS 114 -> 280 -> 288 -> 350 -> 435/341) and the math spine (MATH 211 -> 222/335 -> capstone) progress sensibly, and a deepest prereq chain of 7 shows real layering. Two problems hold it to adequate: the parsed plan starts at 'Second Year - 1st Semester', so the entire first-year foundation (intro CS, Calc I/II, intro physics) is missing; and the shown plan is conspicuously physics-heavy (PHYS 234/335/430/432/442/485, OPSE 310) for a degree titled CS + Applied Mathematics, so the applied-math half is underrepresented in the visible plan of study.
dimensions.suggestion: Add the missing first-year term and explicitly label which courses satisfy the CS degree versus the Applied Mathematics degree so the dual-degree progression is legible end to end.

dimensions.name: Prerequisite structure health
dimensions.justification: The prerequisite graph is deep and healthy: a deepest chain of 7 with a clean applied-math ladder (MATH 340 -> 391/331 -> 440/450 -> 451 at depth 7) and well-formed boolean/grade conditions throughout. The 8 dangling references (missingCourseCount 8: CHEM 126A, GER 200, GER 300, MATH 300, MATH 400, PHYS 111A, PHYS 121A, PHYS 231A) are all lab co-sections or GER/elective-tier placeholders rather than genuine prerequisite breaks.
dimensions.suggestion: Map the 8 dangling codes to real catalog entries or explicitly mark GER 200/300 and MATH 300/400 as category placeholders so the graph resolves cleanly.

dimensions.name: Learning outcomes & objectives
dimensions.justification: The provided data contains no program-level learning outcomes or educational objectives, only a plan of study and course list. This absence is a real catalog finding and is especially costly for a dual degree, where the value proposition is the integrated computing-plus-applied-mathematics competency that goes unstated.
dimensions.suggestion: Publish explicit program learning outcomes distinguishing the computing and applied-mathematics competencies the combined degree is designed to produce.

dimensions.name: Credit distribution & balance

As presented the credit distribution across GER, CS core, math, and physics cannot be verified, so this is primarily a data-consistency caveat that nonetheless undermines transparency.
dimensions.suggestion: Include the missing first-year term so summed credits reconcile to 134, then add a category-level breakdown (GER, CS core, applied-math, physics, electives).

dimensions.name: Presentation & clarity
dimensions.justification: Terms are labeled with per-term credit totals, but the page is incomplete and noisy: the first-year term is absent entirely (only 7 requirement groups, starting at Year 2), 'or ...' alternatives (or MATH 328, or COM 313, or R750 404) render as detached credit-less rows, and there is no program-level overview. The combination of a missing term and detached fragments makes the plan hard to read as a whole.
dimensions.suggestion: Restore the first-year term, fold 'or ...' alternatives into their parent requirements, and add an introductory program description.

dimensions.name: Industry & currency relevance
dimensions.justification: The coursework is contemporary and marketable: CS 301 (data science), CS 351 (cybersecurity), CS 356 (networks), CS 435 (algorithms), CS 331 (databases), CS 490/491, plus a strong computational-math core (MATH 340 Applied Numerical Methods, MATH 391 Numerical Linear Algebra, MATH 453 High-Performance Numerical Computing, MATH 448 Stochastic Simulation). The chief gaps are a dedicated machine-learning course and no explicit optimization or cloud/DevOps exposure.
dimensions.suggestion: Add a machine-learning elective and a continuous-optimization course to convert the strong numerical/statistics base into the quantitative-ML skill set employers now expect.

descriptionQuality.justification: Sampled descriptions are consistently present, specific, and substantive (CS 301 on data-science models, CS 341 on automata/computability, MATH 453 on state-of-the-art large-scale numerical algorithms, MATH 448 on stochastic simulation). A minor blemish is bleed-through in MATH 222's excerpt (which runs into unrelated 'MATH 225. Survey of Probability...' text).

prerequisiteClarity.justification: Prerequisites are generally clear with explicit grade conditions and sensible AND/OR logic (e.g., CS 341 '(CS 241 or MATH 226) and CS 280', MATH 450 'MATH 331 and MATH 337 and MATH 340'). Clarity is reduced by the 8 program-level dangling references and by 'or' alternatives appearing as detached null-code items, weakening end-to-end consistency.

missingTopics.rationale: The program has an unusually strong stats and linear-algebra base (MATH 333, MATH 337, MATH 391) plus CS 301 data science, yet no dedicated ML course; adding statistical/machine learning would be the single highest-leverage addition for an applied-math+CS graduate.

Optimization is foundational to applied mathematics, operations research, and ML training, but no optimization course appears despite the deep numerical suite (MATH 340/391/453); add a convex/numerical optimization course.

CS + applied math is a classic feeder to quantitative finance, and with MATH 448 Stochastic Simulation already present a mathematical-finance elective would open a high-demand career path currently unaddressed.

missingTopics.topic: Modern data engineering and big-data tooling
missingTopics.rationale: CS 301 is the only data course; graduates working with the large-scale numerical problems MATH 453 targets need distributed-data and pipeline skills, so a data-engineering elective is warranted.
Beyond CS 241/CS 435, there is no dedicated discrete-optimization or graph-theory course, a gap for applied-math students heading into networks, logistics, and OR.

emergingTrends.trend: Scientific machine learning and AI-augmented solvers
emergingTrends.rationale: ML surrogates and neural solvers are reshaping numerical computing; the program's numerical suite (MATH 340/391/440/453) is an ideal launchpad for a scientific-ML pathway.
MATH 453 already introduces HPC numerical algorithms; formalizing GPU/parallel computing would match where large-scale applied-math workloads now run.
With CS theory (CS 241/CS 341) plus deep applied math, the program is well placed to add quantum-algorithm content as the area matures.
Stochastic simulation (MATH 448) plus CS gives a strong quant foundation; ML-driven trading and risk modeling are a natural, marketable direction.
Combining the program's PDE/numerical courses with statistics points toward UQ, increasingly central to simulation-based science and engineering.

topSuggestions: Reconcile the credit plan by restoring the missing first-year term so summed credits meet the stated 134 (currently 101, delta -33), then publish a category-level credit breakdown.
Publish explicit program learning outcomes distinguishing the computing and applied-mathematics competencies of the combined degree.
Add a machine-learning elective and a continuous-optimization course to turn the strong numerical/statistics base into a modern quantitative-ML skill set.
Clarify the dual-degree structure by labeling which courses satisfy CS versus Applied Mathematics, since the visible plan reads as physics-heavy relative to the program title.

Fix presentation and resolve the 8 dangling references: fold 'or ...' alternatives into their parent rows, add a program overview, and map or flag CHEM 126A, PHYS 111A/121A/231A, GER 200/300, and MATH 300/400.

b-s-in-data-science-268.name: B.S. in Data Science

dimensions.name: Curriculum coherence & progression
dimensions.justification: The 8-term plan is a model of ordered progression: a programming spine (CS 100 -> CS 113 -> CS 114 -> CS 241/CS 280 -> CS 288) runs in parallel with a math/stats track (MATH 111 -> 112 -> 244 Probability / 337 Linear Algebra -> 341 Statistical Methods II -> 478 Stat Methods in Data Science), and both converge into the data core (CS 331 Databases, DS 340, CS 370 AI, CS 375 ML, CS 482 Data Mining, CS 444 Big Data Systems, CS 450 Data Visualization) and a two-part capstone (CS 492/DS 493). Prerequisite depths climb smoothly from 0 to 7 with no abrupt jumps.
dimensions.suggestion: Convert the generic 'Data Science Elective 1/2/3' placeholders into named concentration tracks (e.g., ML systems, business analytics, bio/health data) so the upper-level path is as prescribed as the core.

dimensions.name: Prerequisite structure health
dimensions.justification: The prerequisite graph is well-shaped: deepestPrereqChain=7 is appropriate for a quantitative four-year degree, and the 6 dangling references (missingCourseCount=6: GER 200, GER 300, PHYS 111A, PHYS 121A, YWCC 207, YWCC 307) are GER placeholders, physics labs, and one-credit seminars rather than genuine data-science prerequisite breaks. Structural health is strong but not perfectly clean.
dimensions.suggestion: Add catalog entries or code mappings for YWCC 207/307 and the PHYS 111A/121A labs and resolve the GER 200/300 placeholders so no referenced code dangles.

dimensions.name: Learning outcomes & objectives
dimensions.justification: No program-level learning outcomes or educational objectives appear anywhere in the provided data; only a term-by-term plan and per-course descriptions are present. For a flagship B.S. this absence is a real catalog finding.
dimensions.suggestion: Publish explicit program learning outcomes (e.g., what a graduate can model, engineer, analyze, visualize, and communicate) on the catalog page.

dimensions.name: Credit distribution & balance
dimensions.justification: The plan does not reconcile: statedTotalCredits is null and creditReconcileDelta is therefore null, while summedTermCredits is only 105.0, well short of a typical ~120-credit B.S. The eighth term (Fourth Year, 2nd Semester) is truncated with null credits and just two items (a senior-seminar GER and one free elective), so both the stated total and the final-term accounting are incomplete.
dimensions.suggestion: State the program's total credit requirement and complete the eighth-term listing so the summed credits reconcile to a stated total (targeting the ~120-121 typical of a B.S.).

dimensions.name: Presentation & clarity
dimensions.justification: Terms are clearly labeled by year and semester and the required courses are legible, but presentation is undercut by the truncated final term (null credits, only two rows), the null stated total, and repeated generic 'Data Science Elective' placeholders that leave the upper-level path visually open.
dimensions.suggestion: Complete the eighth-term rows, add a stated total, and replace bare elective placeholders with example course lists so the plan is fully legible end to end.

dimensions.name: Industry & currency relevance
dimensions.justification: The required core is strong and current for data science: CS 375 Machine Learning, CS 482 Data Mining, CS 444 Big Data Systems, CS 450 Data Visualization, CS 370 AI, CS 331 Databases, and a deep statistics track (MATH 244/341/478). The main currency gaps are the absence of a dedicated deep-learning course beyond intro ML, no MLOps/data-engineering pipeline course, and no explicit generative-AI/LLM content.
dimensions.suggestion: Add a deep-learning course and an MLOps/data-engineering course so graduates can both build modern models and deploy them in production.

descriptionQuality.justification: Course descriptions are present and substantive across the program's spine (CS 375 Machine Learning, CS 444 Big Data Systems, CS 482 Data Mining, MATH 478 Statistical Methods in Data Science), with no thin or placeholder excerpts observed among sampled courses.

prerequisiteClarity.justification: Prerequisites are stated with clear course logic and grade conditions, and the only dangling references (missingCourseCount=6) are non-core GER placeholders, physics labs, and one-credit seminars rather than data-science course breaks, so the core prerequisite chain is unambiguous.

missingTopics.rationale: CS 375 is 'Introduction to Machine Learning' and CS 370 is intro AI, but there is no dedicated deep-learning course (CNNs, RNNs, transformers), which is now foundational to contemporary data science.

CS 444 Big Data Systems is infrastructure-adjacent, but no course covers data pipelines, feature stores, model deployment, or monitoring, the single biggest skills gap for industry data-science hiring.
The stats spine (MATH 244/341/478) is strong on probability and regression but has no A/B testing or causal inference, which are central to product and industry analytics.
CS 482 Data Mining and CS 370 AI touch adjacent ground, but there is no text/NLP or computer-vision course even though most modern data is unstructured.

missingTopics.topic: Responsible AI: fairness, privacy, and interpretability
missingTopics.rationale: IS 350 Computers, Society and Ethics is a general ethics course; given the ML and data-mining core, the program should add dedicated algorithmic-fairness, privacy (e.g., differential privacy), and model-interpretability content.

emergingTrends.trend: Generative AI and large language models
emergingTrends.rationale: The AI/ML spine (CS 370/375/482) predates the foundation-model era; a course or module on LLMs, prompting, retrieval-augmented generation, and fine-tuning is essential positioning for graduates.
Industry has shifted from model-building to reliable ML systems; CS 444 and the CS 492/DS 493 capstone could anchor a production-ML track.
CS 444 Big Data Systems should modernize toward streaming (Kafka), lakehouse architectures, and cloud data platforms that dominate current data engineering.
Regulation such as the EU AI Act and practices like model cards are arriving; graduates need fairness, privacy, and governance literacy beyond the single IS 350 ethics course.

The elective pool already includes BME 210 and BNFO 135 (bio/health signals and bioinformatics); deepening domain tracks (health, business analytics) via the elective placeholders would sharpen employability.

topSuggestions: State a total credit requirement and complete the truncated eighth term so the plan reconciles (current summed credits are only 105 against a null stated total).
Add a deep-learning course and an MLOps/data-engineering course to close the two clearest gaps between the curriculum and industry data-science practice.
Introduce generative-AI/LLM content and a responsible-AI (fairness/privacy/interpretability) offering to modernize the AI-ML core beyond IS 350's general ethics.
Convert the 'Data Science Elective 1/2/3' placeholders into named concentration tracks with example course lists.
Publish program-level learning outcomes and resolve the 6 dangling GER/lab/seminar references so the catalog page is complete.

b-s-in-human-computer-interaction-247.name: B.S. in Human-Computer Interaction

dimensions.name: Curriculum coherence & progression
dimensions.justification: The plan builds a genuinely coherent HCI identity: a UX research spine (IS 247 Designing the User Experience -> IS 375 Discovering User Needs -> IS 448 Usability & Measuring UX), a psychology/methods track (PSY 210 plus cross-listed R830 Cognitive Processes, Statistical Methods, and Experimental Methods), a human-factors requirement (IE 355 or AD 201), a design intro (AD 150), and a technical base (CS 100, IS 117, IS 218, IS 331) converging on an IS 491/IT 491 capstone. Progression is well-ordered. The weakness is that the technical/build side stops at web development (IS 218) with no interaction-implementation course, so the degree leans research-heavy relative to making.
dimensions.suggestion: Insert a prototyping/interaction-design studio between IS 247 and IS 448 so students who learn to research and evaluate interfaces also learn to build interactive prototypes, not only web pages.

dimensions.name: Prerequisite structure health
dimensions.justification: The prerequisite graph is sensible: deepestPrereqChain is 7 (IS 448 Usability & Measuring UX, an appropriate terminal for the UX research sequence) with foundational courses at shallow depths and no evidence of over-constraining. missingCourseCount is 3 and all are peripheral -- GER 300 is a general-education placeholder and YWCC 207/YWCC 307 are one-credit professional-development seminars listed in the plan but absent from the course catalog.
dimensions.suggestion: Resolve the three dangling references by cataloguing YWCC 207/YWCC 307 and re-tagging GER 300 as a requirement category rather than a course code.

dimensions.name: Learning outcomes & objectives
dimensions.justification: No program-level learning outcomes, objectives, or overview are present in the data. For an interdisciplinary HCI degree spanning computing, psychology, and design, the absence of an explicit competency statement is especially costly because a student cannot see how the three strands are meant to add up.
dimensions.suggestion: Publish 5-8 learning outcomes covering user research, interaction/interface design, usability evaluation, cognitive/psychological foundations, and ethical design, plus a short overview of the computing/psychology/design blend.

dimensions.name: Credit distribution & balance
dimensions.justification: The plan reconciles (statedTotalCredits 120, summedTermCredits 120, creditReconcileDelta 0.0) and term loads are steady at 14-16 credits. However, transparency is weakened by heavy reliance on unspecified placeholders -- four 'HCI Specialization Electives' plus roughly eight 'General Elective' slots -- and by cross-institution R830 psychology courses whose 3-4 credit values sit oddly against the otherwise uniform 3-credit rows, making the true required content harder to read.
dimensions.suggestion: Enumerate the four HCI specialization electives (or link an approved list) and label the R830 psychology courses' credit values explicitly so the balance between required HCI content and open electives is visible.

dimensions.name: Presentation & clarity
dimensions.justification: Terms are labeled and the core HCI courses render cleanly, but the cross-listed Rutgers-Newark psychology courses appear as null-code title rows with registrar abbreviations ('R830 301 Stat Meth Cog & Beh', 'R830 304 Cognitive Processes', 'R830 102 Prin Of Psychology'), several 'Select one of the following:' and 'or ...' options are unresolved lines, and GER rows are split with null credits.
dimensions.suggestion: Give the R830 cross-listed courses proper codes/titles (or map them to NJIT equivalents), and collapse the 'Select one of the following' and 'or ...' groupings into single readable choice lines.

dimensions.name: Industry & currency relevance
dimensions.justification: The curriculum reflects current UX practice strongly: dedicated user-research (IS 375), usability and UX measurement (IS 448), human factors (IE 355), and a solid cognitive/experimental-methods foundation give graduates the research toolkit UX teams hire for. The visible gaps are modern-tooling and craft rather than concept: there is no explicit accessibility course, no prototyping/design-systems studio (Figma), and no front-end implementation depth, so the degree is stronger on evaluating experiences than on designing and shipping them.
dimensions.suggestion: Add an accessibility/inclusive-design course and a prototyping studio using current tools (Figma, design systems) to complement the strong research core with production-ready design skills.

descriptionQuality.justification: All 24 sampled courses carry present, informative descriptions with no placeholders; the HCI core is especially rich (IS 247 on interface design/evaluation, IS 375 framed around anticipating user needs, IS 448 on UX research and usability, IE 355 human-machine systems). A handful of GER/math rows are terser, but no description reads as thin or empty.

prerequisiteClarity.justification: Prerequisites are stated with clear grade gates and sensible logic (IS 218: '(IS 117 or IT 202) and (CS 100, CS 113, or CS 115)'; COM 312/313 with explicit ENGL 102 + GER conditions), and intro courses correctly show none. Clarity is dinged only by the cross-listed R830 psychology courses, whose prerequisites are not surfaced in NJIT terms, and by minor phrasing variance.

missingTopics.rationale: No course addresses accessibility, yet it is now a legal and professional baseline for UX; it should be a required complement to IS 247/IS 448 rather than absent.
The program teaches research and evaluation (IS 375/IS 448) but no dedicated hands-on interaction-design/prototyping course, leaving graduates strong on 'why' but light on 'how to build'.
Technical build stops at IS 218 web development; HCI graduates increasingly need to prototype interactive, stateful interfaces, not static pages.

missingTopics.topic: Quantitative UX / experimentation and A/B testing at scale
missingTopics.rationale: IS 448 covers usability measurement, but modern product UX relies on large-sample experimentation and behavioral analytics that the current lab-oriented R830 stats track does not reach.
A core HCI competency for structuring complex systems is not explicitly represented among the IS/design courses.

emergingTrends.trend: Designing for generative AI and human-AI interaction
emergingTrends.rationale: Prompt UX, conversational flows, and trust/uncertainty in AI features are the fastest-growing UX area; the program's strong research core (IS 375/IS 448) is ideally placed to add an AI-interaction elective.
Voice and multimodal UX are mainstream in consumer and enterprise products, extending the interface-design scope of IS 247 beyond screens.
Spatial interfaces are a major near-future direction for HCI; a specialization elective here would future-proof the design track.
Building on IS 350 ethics, ethical/persuasive-design scrutiny is now central to UX hiring and regulation.
UX research is professionalizing into ops and analytics-driven practice, a natural extension of IS 375/IS 448 into tooling and repositories.

topSuggestions: Publish explicit HCI learning outcomes and a program overview that makes the computing/psychology/design blend legible.
Add a required accessibility/inclusive-design course -- the single most conspicuous gap for a modern UX degree.
Add an interaction-design/prototyping studio (Figma, design systems) plus front-end prototyping to balance the research-heavy core.
Introduce a 'designing for AI / conversational interaction' elective that leverages the strong existing UX-research sequence.
Enumerate the HCI specialization electives and clean the cross-listed R830 psychology rows so required content is visible.

b-s-in-information-technology-254.name: B.S. in Information Technology

dimensions.name: Curriculum coherence & progression
dimensions.justification: This is the most technically coherent of the informatics degrees: a programming arc (CS 100 -> CS 113 -> IT 114 Advanced Programming), a networking/systems arc (IT 120 -> IT 340 System Administration and IT 420 Computer Systems & Networks -> IT 490 Systems Integration), a data arc (IS 331 database), and design/apps (IT 201, IT 202) all converge on an IT 491 capstone. Term loads are steady at 13-16 credits and difficulty ramps sensibly. The only softness is the large block of eight generic 'Specialization Course' slots whose sequencing/content is not shown, so the concentration's internal progression is invisible.
dimensions.suggestion: Publish the named specialization tracks with their ordered course lists so the eight 'Specialization Course' slots read as a coherent concentration rather than interchangeable placeholders.

dimensions.name: Prerequisite structure health
dimensions.justification: The prerequisite graph is well-formed and slightly shallower than its IS siblings: deepestPrereqChain is 6 (IT 490 Systems Integration, gated on CS 113/115, IS 331/CS 331, and IT 340 -- a sensible terminal), with clear intermediate chains (IT 340/IT 420 on IT 120 or CS 356). missingCourseCount is 4 and all are peripheral -- GER 200/GER 300 placeholders and the one-credit YWCC 207/YWCC 307 seminars.
dimensions.suggestion: Resolve the four dangling references (catalog YWCC 207/YWCC 307; re-tag GER 200/GER 300 as requirement categories) so the otherwise clean IT prerequisite graph has no loose ends.

dimensions.name: Learning outcomes & objectives
dimensions.justification: No program-level learning outcomes, objectives, or overview are present in the data. Given IT's ABET-style emphasis on applied competencies, the absence of a stated outcomes list is a meaningful catalog gap.
dimensions.suggestion: Publish 5-8 learning outcomes covering programming, networking/system administration, database, systems integration, and professional practice, plus a short program overview.

dimensions.name: Credit distribution & balance
dimensions.justification: Credit accounting reconciles cleanly (statedTotalCredits 120, summedTermCredits 120, creditReconcileDelta 0.0), and the required IT core is substantial and well-defined (IT 101/114/120/201/202/340/420/490 plus CS 100/113, IS 331/350). The transparency caveat is the large open block -- eight 'Specialization Courses' plus roughly seven 'General Electives' -- which is a lot of undefined credit even though the core anchors the degree.
dimensions.suggestion: Show a credit-distribution summary and label the specialization block's credit total so the split between the defined IT core and the ~24 credits of specialization/free electives is explicit.

dimensions.name: Presentation & clarity
dimensions.justification: Cleaner than the IS B.A. -- fewer footnote artifacts -- but the same class of defects remains: GER rows are split with null credits ('Science Literacy GER', 'History and Humanities GER 300+ level'), the COM alternative renders as a null-code 'or COM 312 or Oral Presentations' line, eight identical 'Specialization Course' placeholders carry no content, and YWCC titles are abbreviated ('Computing & Effective Com').
dimensions.suggestion: Collapse the GER and 'or ...' rows into single readable lines and either name or link the eight specialization slots so the plan is not dominated by identical placeholders.

dimensions.name: Industry & currency relevance
dimensions.justification: The core covers durable IT skills -- programming (IT 114), networking (IT 120/IT 420), system administration (IT 340), internet applications (IT 202), systems integration (IT 490), and database (IS 331) -- but it reads as traditional on-premises IT. The single most conspicuous gap is the absence of any cybersecurity course despite a networking-and-sysadmin backbone that is the ideal foundation for it; there is also no cloud/DevOps, virtualization/containers, or infrastructure-automation content, all now central to IT roles.
dimensions.suggestion: Add a required cybersecurity course (network/systems security) and a cloud-computing/DevOps course, so the networking + sysadmin base leads into the security and cloud skills employers now demand.

descriptionQuality.justification: All 19 sampled courses carry present, specific descriptions with no missing/placeholder entries (e.g., IT 340 system administration, IT 420 computer systems and networks, IT 490 systems integration, IS 331 database design). Content is informative and course-appropriate; only a few registrar titles are terse.

prerequisiteClarity.justification: Prerequisites are stated with explicit grade gates and clean alternatives (IT 114: 'CS 113 or CS 115'; IT 202: 'CS 100 or CS 113 or CS 115 or a course in a high-level programming language as approved by department'; IT 490: 'CS 113 or CS 115, IS 331 or CS 331, and IT 340'), and intro courses correctly show none. The consistent, well-formed chains make this the clearest prerequisite set among the informatics degrees; minor phrasing variance keeps it from a 5.

missingTopics.rationale: A program built on networking (IT 120/IT 420) and system administration (IT 340) with no security course has a glaring gap; security is now inseparable from every IT operations role and should be a required course.

missingTopics.topic: Cloud computing and virtualization (AWS/Azure, containers, Kubernetes)
missingTopics.rationale: IT 340/IT 420/IT 490 are on-premises-centric; modern IT infrastructure is cloud and container based, so a cloud/virtualization course is essential currency.
IT 114 teaches application programming, but operational automation (infrastructure-as-code, pipelines) that defines contemporary IT work is absent.
Systems integration (IT 490) would benefit from ITSM practices and modern monitoring/observability that IT operations roles rely on daily.
Enterprise IT increasingly manages mobile and IoT endpoints, a natural extension of the networking/sysadmin core not currently represented.

emergingTrends.rationale: The sysadmin/systems-integration roles this program trains for are shifting to cloud-native DevOps/SRE; IT 340/IT 490 should evolve toward automated, cloud-based operations.
Security is now central to every IT function; the program's networking + sysadmin base is the ideal launchpad for a zero-trust-oriented security track.
LLM-assisted troubleshooting and automated incident response are reshaping IT operations, a forward-looking addition to IT 490 systems integration.
Declarative, versioned infrastructure (Terraform/Ansible) is now standard practice and a direct modernization of the IT 340 system-administration content.
Distributed edge/IoT infrastructure extends the networking curriculum (IT 120/IT 420) into a fast-growing operational domain.

topSuggestions: Add a required cybersecurity course -- the largest gap given the networking and system-administration backbone.
Add cloud-computing/DevOps with virtualization/containers and infrastructure-as-code to modernize the on-prem-centric core.
Publish program-level learning outcomes and an overview.
Name and sequence the eight 'Specialization Course' slots into published tracks so the concentration's progression is visible.
Clean presentation: collapse split GER and 'or ...' rows and replace identical specialization placeholders with real course listings.

b-s-in-science-technology-society-and-b-.name: B.S. in Science, Technology & Society and B.S. in Business and Information Systems

dimensions.name: Curriculum coherence & progression
dimensions.justification: The information-systems spine is coherent and genuinely vertical (CS 100->113->114, IS 117->218->219->421, IS 331->IS 491 senior project) with a deepestPrereqChain of 7, and business courses (ACCT 115/117, ECON 201, FIN 218/315, HRM 301, ENTR 210, IE 492) interleave sensibly. However, only one plan-of-study term parsed (requirementGroups=1, the '2nd Semester' block), so term-over-term ordering cannot be verified, and for a dual degree whose first half is Science, Technology & Society the STS/policy/social-studies-of-technology content is almost invisible in the course set, represented only by IS 350 Computers, Society and Ethics.
dimensions.suggestion: Restore the full eight-term plan-of-study grid and explicitly slot the STS half (history/philosophy of science, technology policy, science-and-society courses) so the dual degree reads as an integrated program rather than a BIS track with a missing companion.

dimensions.name: Prerequisite structure health
dimensions.justification: The prerequisite graph is well-formed and readable, with grade-conditioned prerequisites (e.g., 'CS 113 with a grade C or better', 'IS 219 and (IS 331 or CS 331)') and per-course depths from 0 to 7. missingCourseCount is only 3, and all three (YWCC 107, YWCC 207, YWCC 307) are one-credit college seminars that dangle without breaking any core IS chain; the depth-7 path into IS 465/IS 448 is on the deep side but genuine.
dimensions.suggestion: Catalog the three YWCC 107/207/307 seminars so they resolve, and audit the depth-7 chain into IS 465 (statistics GUR -> ... ) to confirm it does not over-constrain scheduling in an already dual-major load.

dimensions.name: Learning outcomes & objectives
dimensions.justification: The program data contains no program-level learning outcomes, educational objectives, or narrative overview of any kind; only the course list and a single parsed term are present. For a dual degree that fuses a humanities/social-science field (STS) with an applied IS field, the absence of outcomes is especially costly because the integrative value proposition is left entirely implicit.
dimensions.suggestion: Publish 5-7 program-level learning outcomes that explicitly bridge the two halves (e.g., analyze the societal and ethical impact of information systems, design and build business web/mobile applications, evaluate technology policy with data).

dimensions.name: Credit distribution & balance
dimensions.justification: The plan does not reconcile: summedTermCredits is only 12.0 against a statedTotalCredits of 120.0, giving a creditReconcileDelta of -108.0 because just one 12-credit term parsed. Beyond the parsing gap, 120 credits is itself suspect for a genuine dual B.S./B.S. degree, which typically requires substantially more, so the reader cannot see how general-education, STS, business, and IS credits are apportioned.
dimensions.suggestion: Rebuild the full credit grid and confirm the true total for the dual degree, then add a distribution summary (general education / STS core / business core / IS core / electives) so the 120 (or corrected) figure is transparent and reconciles term by term.

dimensions.name: Presentation & clarity
dimensions.justification: Only one term rendered (the '2nd Semester' group), several rows carry null course codes, and alternatives appear as bare text such as 'or IT 491 or IT Capstone Project'; there is no program description, no outcomes, and no STS-side structure. The single parsed block therefore reads as a fragment rather than a catalog-quality plan.
dimensions.suggestion: Fix the plan-of-study parsing so all terms render with labeled headings and per-term credit totals, collapse 'or' alternatives into clear either/or pairs, and add a short program overview describing the STS+BIS pairing.

dimensions.name: Industry & currency relevance
dimensions.justification: The BIS half is current and employable (IS 218 web apps, IS 322 mobile, IS 331 databases, IS 333 social network analysis, IS 390 requirements analysis, IS 455 IS management, IT 310 e-commerce, IS 247/375 UX), but for a program explicitly branded Science, Technology & Society the currency of the STS half is thin: only IS 350 touches ethics, and there is no data governance, technology policy, privacy law, or applied data-science/AI content despite this being exactly where STS+IS careers now concentrate.
dimensions.suggestion: Add or list electives in technology policy / data governance and applied data science or AI so the STS half carries modern currency (algorithmic accountability, privacy regulation) rather than resting on a single ethics course.

descriptionQuality.justification: The sampled courses carry real, specific descriptions rather than placeholders (e.g., ACCT 115 develops 'fundamentals of financial accounting'; IS 333 teaches programs that 'grab information from social networking systems ... and analyze this to reveal useful but hidden information'). Excerpts are truncated at ~220 characters, and a few are genuinely thin (IS 489 INFO Undergrad Thesis Research at ~81 characters), but coverage is otherwise strong and informative.

prerequisiteClarity.justification: Prerequisites are stated in clear, human-readable form with explicit grade conditions and boolean structure (e.g., '(IS 117 or IT 202) and (CS 100, CS 113, or CS 115)'), and the parser resolved a clean depth graph. The only blemish is the 3 dangling YWCC 107/207/307 seminar references, which are peripheral one-credit courses rather than gatekeepers.

missingTopics.topic: Technology and science policy / STS methods
missingTopics.rationale: For a degree whose first half is Science, Technology & Society, the course set shows no policy analysis, history/philosophy of science, or qualitative social-science methods course; IS 350 alone cannot carry the STS half, so a dedicated STS core (e.g., a Technology, Policy and Society course) is needed.

missingTopics.topic: Applied data analytics and machine learning
missingTopics.rationale: The IS track stops at IS 333 social network analysis and IS 392 web mining; there is no applied ML, predictive modeling, or Python/R data-science course, which is now standard for information-systems graduates and directly relevant to an STS student studying data-driven society.

No cybersecurity, privacy, or data-governance course appears despite IS 218/322/331 building data-collecting web, mobile, and database systems; STS+BIS graduates working at the technology-society boundary need GDPR/CCPA-style privacy and security engineering literacy.

Only MATH 105 (elementary probability and statistics) is visible; an STS+IS program that analyzes technology in society should require inferential statistics and research design (e.g., MATH 333) so students can produce, not just consume, empirical claims.

The web sequence (IS 117/218/219/421) is traditional server-side web development with no cloud, DevOps, or API/microservice content, leaving a gap between the program's build courses and how business information systems are actually deployed today.

emergingTrends.rationale: This is the fastest-growing STS-meets-technology niche and the natural destination for STS+BIS graduates; the program should extend IS 350 into a dedicated responsible-AI / algorithmic-accountability course to own this space.

Privacy law (GDPR, CCPA, state AI acts) is reshaping how information systems are built and operated; a program that combines societal analysis with hands-on IS development is ideally positioned to teach privacy-by-design if it adds a privacy/compliance elective.

LLM-assisted development and AI-embedded business software are now baseline industry practice; IS 218/322/421 build courses should incorporate AI-assisted and AI-feature development so graduates ship current systems.

emergingTrends.trend: Platform and social-media analytics for policy
emergingTrends.rationale: IS 333 social network analysis is a strong foundation the program can grow into misinformation, platform governance, and computational social science, uniting the STS and IS halves around a marketable, high-demand skill set.

With IS 247/375/448 already present, the program can position for value-sensitive and ethical design, an area where STS framing plus UX practice is a distinctive combination employers increasingly seek.

topSuggestions: Rebuild the full plan-of-study grid; only one 12-credit term parsed (creditReconcileDelta -108 against a stated 120), so students currently cannot see the degree structure at all.
Make the Science, Technology & Society half explicit by slotting STS/policy/history-of-science courses, since the visible curriculum is almost entirely BIS with only IS 350 representing STS.
Publish program-level learning outcomes that bridge societal analysis and applied IS, none of which currently appear in the data.
Add modern data and policy currency: an applied data-science/ML course and a technology-policy or data-privacy course, closing the biggest STS-side and IS-side gaps at once.
Catalog the YWCC 107/207/307 seminars and verify the true dual-degree credit total so the page reconciles and dangling references disappear.

b-s-in-web-information-systems-258.name: B.S. in Web & Information Systems
