Coherence is materially hurt, however, by the plan being truncated: only 7 requirement groups parsed and the Fourth Year 2nd Semester -- the culminating term and any capstone (IS 491) -- is entirely absent, so the progression does not visibly finish.
dimensions.suggestion: Restore the missing Fourth Year 2nd Semester, including the capstone, so the strong web-development sequence reads as a completed four-year progression rather than stopping mid-senior-year.

dimensions.name: Prerequisite structure health
dimensions.justification: The prerequisite graph is sensible and notably rich (75 referenced courses): deepestPrereqChain is 7 (IS 333 Social Network Analysis, gated on computing and statistical GERs), with the web sequence forming clean intermediate chains (IS 322: 'IS 218, IS 219, or IT 202'; IS 421 advanced web on the IS 218/219 base). missingCourseCount is 4 and all are peripheral -- GER 200/GER 300 placeholders and the one-credit YWCC 207/YWCC 307 seminars.
dimensions.suggestion: Resolve the four dangling references (catalog YWCC 207/YWCC 307; re-tag GER 200/GER 300 as requirement categories) so the deep, well-formed web/IS graph has no loose ends.

dimensions.name: Learning outcomes & objectives
dimensions.justification: No program-level learning outcomes, objectives, or overview are present in the data, so a prospective student cannot see what distinguishes 'Web & Information Systems' from the general IS degree beyond the course titles themselves.
dimensions.suggestion: Publish learning outcomes emphasizing full-stack web development, content/data-driven web systems, UX, and web analytics, plus a short overview positioning the degree against the general B.S./B.A. in IS.

dimensions.name: Credit distribution & balance
dimensions.justification: Credit accounting cannot be reconciled: statedTotalCredits is null, creditReconcileDelta is null, and summedTermCredits is only 108 -- roughly a full term (~12 credits) short of a typical 120-credit B.S. This directly reflects the missing Fourth Year 2nd Semester, so both the program total and the final-term credits are unaccounted for. This is a genuine credit-transparency defect and a data-consistency caveat, not merely a rounding issue.
dimensions.suggestion: Parse and display a single stated total credit count and restore the missing final term so the summed term credits reconcile to it (currently null total and only 108 summed credits).

dimensions.name: Presentation & clarity
dimensions.justification: The most serious presentation defect is the truncated plan -- only seven terms render and the final semester is missing entirely -- which alone undermines completeness. On top of that, GER rows are split with null credits, alternatives render as null-code 'or COM 313 or Technical Writing' lines, footnote numbers bleed into titles ('General Elective 3 2'), and several titles are abbreviated ('IS 219 Adv Website Development', 'Computing & Effective Com'), so the page reads as incomplete and noisy.
dimensions.suggestion: Restore the missing final term first, then merge split GER and 'or ...' rows, strip footnote numbers from titles, and expand truncated course titles.

dimensions.name: Industry & currency relevance
dimensions.justification: This is the most web-current of the informatics degrees: a genuine full web progression (IS 117/218/219/421) plus mobile applications (IS 322), content management systems (IS 373), web mining and information retrieval (IS 392), and social network analysis (IS 333) map well onto contemporary web-engineering and web-data roles. The gaps are modern-stack specifics rather than concept: no explicit modern JavaScript-framework/API course, no web/application security, and no cloud/serverless deployment, so graduates may be strong on breadth but light on the exact production stack.
dimensions.suggestion: Add a modern full-stack course (JavaScript framework + REST/GraphQL APIs) and a web-application-security course, and update IS 219/IS 421 to reflect current framework and deployment practice.

descriptionQuality.justification: All 38 sampled courses carry present, specific descriptions with no missing/placeholder entries across a broad set (IS web courses, CS foundations, DD design, COM communication, IS 333 social-network analysis, IS 392 web mining). The breadth of well-described electives is a strength; only a few registrar titles are terse.

prerequisiteClarity.justification: Prerequisites are stated with explicit grade gates and coherent alternatives across the web sequence (IS 218/IS 219: '(IS 117 or IT 202) and (CS 100, CS 113, or CS 115)'; IS 322: 'IS 218, IS 219, or IT 202'; CS 114: 'CS 113 with a grade C or better'), and intro courses correctly show none. Minor issues -- GER-set placeholders inside some prerequisites (IS 333/IS 350) and phrasing variance -- keep it from a 5.

missingTopics.topic: Web and application security (OWASP, authentication, secure coding)
missingTopics.rationale: A web-development-heavy program (IS 218/219/421, IS 373) with no security course is a notable gap; secure web coding is a baseline expectation for any web engineer.

missingTopics.topic: Modern JavaScript frameworks and full-stack APIs (React/Node, REST/GraphQL)
missingTopics.rationale: The web sequence should explicitly teach current front-end frameworks and API design rather than leaving IS 218/219/421 to generic 'web application' framing.

missingTopics.topic: Cloud/serverless deployment and web DevOps (CI/CD, containers, CDN)
missingTopics.rationale: Building web apps without teaching how to deploy and operate them (IS 421 stops at 'advanced web applications') leaves a real production gap.

missingTopics.topic: Web data engineering (NoSQL, caching, APIs at scale)
missingTopics.rationale: IS 331 covers relational databases only; web systems increasingly rely on NoSQL, caching, and API-driven data layers not represented here.

emergingTrends.trend: Generative AI in web systems (LLM features, RAG, AI-assisted content)
emergingTrends.rationale: AI-powered search, content, and personalization build directly on IS 373 (CMS) and IS 392 (web mining/IR), positioning graduates for the fastest-growing web capability.

emergingTrends.trend: API-first / headless CMS and JAMstack architectures
emergingTrends.rationale: IS 373 Content Management Systems should modernize toward headless/API-first delivery, the dominant contemporary web-content pattern.
IS 322 mobile applications is well placed to cover PWAs and cross-platform frameworks as web and mobile converge.

emergingTrends.trend: Social and graph analytics with recommendation systems
emergingTrends.rationale: IS 333 (social network analysis) and IS 392 (web mining) give the program a strong base to teach recommendation and graph-analytics at scale, a core web-data direction.

emergingTrends.trend: Privacy-first and decentralized web (Web3, consent-driven data)
emergingTrends.rationale: With IT 310 e-commerce and IS 333 present, the program can address privacy-by-design and emerging decentralized-web patterns reshaping web data practice.

topSuggestions: Restore the missing Fourth Year 2nd Semester (including the capstone) and fix credit accounting: statedTotalCredits and creditReconcileDelta are null and summed credits are only 108.
Add a web/application-security course -- the clearest gap in a web-development-focused degree.
Add a modern full-stack course (JavaScript framework + REST/GraphQL APIs) and update IS 219/IS 421 to current framework and deployment practice.
Publish program-level learning outcomes and an overview that positions Web & IS against the general IS degrees.
Add accessibility and cloud/serverless deployment content, then clean the split GER/'or ...' rows and footnote-laden titles.

business-and-information-systems-minor-f.name: Business and Information Systems Minor (for Computing Sciences Majors)

For CS majors, courses that overlap their own major (IS 218/219 web development, IS 331 databases duplicating CS 331, IT 114 programming) add little new, and the business content that should distinguish a BIS minor for technologists is not visible in the pool.
dimensions.suggestion: Differentiate this variant by excluding courses that overlap the computing major and foregrounding business and management content (accounting, economics, finance, IS management, e-commerce) so CS majors gain genuine business breadth rather than repeating technical material.

The deepestPrereqChain is 7, but unlike the non-majors variant that depth is appropriate for this audience: computing-sciences majors have already completed the CS 113/114 and statistics prerequisites, so the depth-5-to-7 electives (IS 421, IS 465) are genuinely reachable rather than blocking.

dimensions.name: Learning outcomes & objectives
dimensions.justification: No program-level learning outcomes or objectives appear in the data. For a minor whose value is giving computing majors business and information-systems breadth, the absence of stated outcomes obscures exactly what differentiates it from the core CS major.
dimensions.suggestion: Publish 4-5 outcomes emphasizing the business/IS competencies CS majors gain (systems analysis, IS management, e-commerce, business process design) that their major does not already cover.

dimensions.name: Credit distribution & balance

dimensions.suggestion: Publish the 15-credit breakdown and an explicit non-overlap rule (credits that count for the CS major cannot double-count here) so the minor demonstrably adds new coursework.

dimensions.name: Presentation & clarity
dimensions.justification: The stated 15-credit total and clean references (missingCourseCount=0) aid clarity, and descriptions/prerequisites are present, but there is no overview, no requirement structure (requirementGroups=0), and nothing on the page that visibly distinguishes this variant from the non-majors minor with which it shares a course pool.
dimensions.suggestion: Add an overview that states the audience and the differentiator versus the non-majors variant, plus an explicit requirement block, so the two 265 minors are not indistinguishable.

dimensions.name: Industry & currency relevance
dimensions.justification: The applied-IS courses are current (IS 218 web apps, IS 322 mobile, IS 331 databases, IT 310 e-commerce, IS 455 IS management), but for computing-sciences majors the differentiating value should be business currency, and the pool underweights business analytics, product/project management, and finance/entrepreneurship that would complement strong technical skills.
dimensions.suggestion: Add business-facing electives (business analytics, product/project management, entrepreneurship/finance) that leverage the student's technical depth, rather than IS courses that restate skills from the CS major.

descriptionQuality.justification: Descriptions are present and specific (e.g., IS 333 social network analysis, IT 265 'core concepts and design methodologies integral to designing and developing games'), informative rather than placeholder. Excerpts are truncated at ~220 characters with a few shorter, but coverage is strong.

prerequisiteClarity.justification: Prerequisites are stated clearly with grade conditions and boolean logic (e.g., '(IS 117 or IT 202) and (CS 100, CS 113, or CS 115)'), and missingCourseCount is 0 with no dangling references, so the graph is internally consistent and, for this CS-major audience, its depth is fully navigable.

missingTopics.rationale: For a BIS minor aimed at computing majors, the highest-value content is business fundamentals their major omits, yet the referenced pool shows applied-IS/IT courses rather than an ACCT/ECON/FIN business core.
CS majors already build software; what a business minor should add is leading it (Agile/Scrum, product, budgeting), and no project-management course is present in the pool.
There is no analytics or decision-modeling course to pair the students' technical ability with business insight, the natural differentiator for technologists adding a business minor.
For CS majors, entrepreneurship/tech-commercialization content converts engineering skill into ventures; it is absent from this minor's visible set despite fitting the audience well.
E-commerce (IT 310) appears, but ERP/enterprise systems and the economics of cloud/SaaS delivery, where business and computing meet, are not represented.

emergingTrends.trend: AI product management and technical leadership
emergingTrends.rationale: Demand is surging for engineers who can lead AI-enabled products; this minor is well placed to add product/technical-leadership content that leverages CS majors' skills.
Computing majors increasingly enter FinTech and platform businesses; e-commerce (IT 310) could anchor a thread toward modern digital business models and payments.
Pairing the students' programming ability with business analytics is a high-return direction the minor should own, rather than restating IS build courses they already know.
Software is delivered as cloud services; teaching the business and economic side of cloud/SaaS gives CS majors context their technical courses omit.
Technology entrepreneurship is a natural, high-demand extension for computing majors; an entrepreneurship elective would make the minor distinctively valuable to this audience.

topSuggestions: Differentiate this variant from the non-majors minor: exclude courses that overlap the CS major (IS 218/219, IS 331, IT 114) and foreground a business core (accounting, economics, finance, management) so CS majors gain genuine business breadth.
Publish an explicit non-overlap/double-counting rule against the CS major so the 15-credit minor demonstrably adds new coursework.
Add product/project-management and business-analytics electives that leverage, rather than repeat, the students' technical skills.
Publish a requirement structure and an overview stating the audience and differentiator; requirementGroups=0 and the shared course pool make this indistinguishable from the non-majors variant today.
Publish learning outcomes emphasizing the business/IS competencies CS majors do not already get from their major.

business-and-information-systems-minor-n.name: Business and Information Systems Minor (not for YWCC majors)

At 15 credits (about five courses) this is a reasonable foundation, but with requirementGroups=0 no required-versus-elective sequence is shown and the pool also carries game/media electives (IT 265/266/286/366/380, DD, AD) that stray from a business-IS focus.

dimensions.suggestion: Add a short minor overview and an explicit requirement block (required courses + 'choose N from' electives) to make the 15-credit structure legible.

dimensions.name: Industry & currency relevance
dimensions.justification: The applied-IS content is current and employable for non-majors (IS 218 web apps, IS 322 mobile, IS 331 databases, IS 247/375 UX, IT 310 e-commerce, IT 120 networking, IT 340 system administration, IS 455 IS management). The gaps are the modern business-technology staples: data analytics/BI, cybersecurity fundamentals, cloud, and project management.
dimensions.suggestion: Add a business-analytics or cybersecurity-fundamentals elective to the menu so non-majors gain a currently in-demand skill alongside the web/database core.

descriptionQuality.justification: Descriptions are present and specific across the sampled courses (e.g., IS 333 social network analysis, IT 265 game architecture and design), with informative content rather than placeholders. prerequisiteClarity.justification: Prerequisites are stated clearly with grade conditions and boolean structure (e.g., '(IS 117 or IT 202) and (CS 100, CS 113, or CS 115)'), and missingCourseCount is 0 with no dangling references, so the graph is internally consistent. computer-science-minor-235.name: Computer Science Minor

dimensions.suggestion: Publish 3-5 learning outcomes stating what a CS minor certifies (e.g., programming proficiency, data-structures fluency, ability to apply computing in the student's major field).

dimensions.name: Credit distribution & balance
dimensions.justification: There is no credit information at all: statedTotalCredits is null, summedTermCredits is null, and creditReconcileDelta is null. Per the rubric this null profile is a data-consistency caveat for a thin-data program rather than evidence of a curriculum imbalance, but as presented a prospective minor cannot see how many credits the minor requires or how they are distributed.
dimensions.suggestion: State the total credit requirement for the minor (typically 15-18 credits) and list exactly which courses count, so the credit load is explicit.

dimensions.name: Presentation & clarity
dimensions.justification: The catalog entry as captured is essentially incomplete: no plan of study (requirementGroups empty), no credit total, no program description, and a course pool that appears to blend CS-minor courses with an unrelated computational-mathematics course set. A reader cannot tell what the minor consists of.
dimensions.suggestion: Render a clean minor page with a one-paragraph description, the required/elective course list, and total credits, and fix the parsing so only genuine CS-minor courses appear.

dimensions.name: Industry & currency relevance
dimensions.justification: The referenced course pool includes current, employable topics (CS 301 Data Science, CS 351 Cybersecurity, CS 388 Android Application Development, CS 331 Databases, CS 435 Algorithms), so a well-chosen minor could be quite marketable. The score is held to adequate because, absent a defined structure, there is no guarantee a minor student actually reaches these modern courses rather than stopping at intro programming.
dimensions.suggestion: Curate a recommended modern elective track for the minor (e.g., a data/AI option and a security option) so non-majors can deliberately pick up in-demand skills.

descriptionQuality.justification: The sampled course descriptions are present, specific, and substantive (CS 388 on the Android SDK and deployment, CS 301 on data-science models and hands-on skills, MATH 453 on state-of-the-art large-scale numerical algorithms), with none reading as thin placeholders; only excerpts are truncated.

prerequisiteClarity.justification: Prerequisites on the referenced courses are stated clearly with explicit grade conditions and sensible OR-alternatives (e.g., CS 280 'CS 114 or CS 116 or IT 114 or equivalent with a grade C or better', CS 388 'CS 288 with a grade C or better'). The main caveat is that some chains (CS 435 via CS 241/CS 288) may be deep for a minor, which the undefined structure does not clarify.

missingTopics.rationale: A CS minor should guarantee programming plus data structures (CS 113/CS 114 and one of CS 241/CS 280), but the data shows no required core, so the most important 'missing topic' is a stated foundation every minor completes.

missingTopics.topic: Applied AI / data literacy for non-majors
missingTopics.rationale: The pool contains CS 301 but no minor-friendly, low-prerequisite applied-AI or data-analytics option, which is exactly what students minoring from other disciplines most want; add an accessible data/AI elective.
CS 388 Android is present but there is no clearly surfaced web or full-stack option scoped for minors, a common motivation for taking a CS minor; curate a practical-development elective.
CS 351 appears in the pool but has deep prerequisites; a minor-accessible security-literacy course would let non-majors gain baseline secure-computing knowledge.

Advanced numerical/applied-math courses (MATH 440/448/450/451/453) appear in the CS-minor pool and are almost certainly mis-scoped from a computational-math program; removing them is as important as adding topics for a coherent minor.

emergingTrends.rationale: A CS minor is the ideal vehicle to give students from other majors practical fluency in building with and reasoning about AI tools; a minor-scoped applied-AI course would be highly attractive.
Minors increasingly serve students combining computing with a home discipline; defining domain-oriented elective tracks (computing for business, science, or design) would modernize the minor.
Data analytics is now a cross-cutting workplace expectation; surfacing an accessible analytics elective positions the minor for demand from non-majors.
As development shifts toward AI-assisted tooling, a minor emphasizing practical, tool-supported building lowers the barrier for non-majors to become productive.
With IS 350 (Computers, Society and Ethics) already in the pool, the minor could foreground responsible-computing literacy as a distinctive, broadly relevant outcome.

topSuggestions: Publish the actual minor requirements and total credits, which are entirely absent from the data (requirementGroups empty; statedTotalCredits, summed, and delta all null).
Define a coherent structure: a small required foundation (CS 113 + CS 114 + one of CS 241/CS 280) plus a curated, minor-accessible elective list.
Fix the course-pool scoping so unrelated advanced computational-mathematics courses (MATH 440/448/450/451/453) are removed from the CS-minor listing.
Publish 3-5 learning outcomes stating what the CS minor certifies to non-majors.
Curate a modern, low-prerequisite elective track (applied AI/data and security literacy) so minor students can deliberately reach in-demand topics rather than stopping at intro programming.

data-analytics-minor-263.name: Data Analytics Minor

dimensions.name: Curriculum coherence & progression
dimensions.justification: A real analytics spine exists in the referenced set: statistics (MATH 333, MATH 341, IE 331), data mining (CS 482), databases (CS 331, IS 331), web mining (IS 392), and social network analysis (IS 333). But that spine is surrounded by many courses with no analytics rationale (AD 112/150 art and design, COM 303/354/355 digital media, DD 284/321, ID 203, HRM 301, ENTR 210, IE 447 legal), and with requirementGroups=0 there is no visible required-core-then-elective ladder to guide selection.
dimensions.suggestion: Define an explicit required analytics core (a statistics course, a databases course, and CS 482 data mining) plus a curated elective list, and drop or clearly separate the art/design/media courses that do not serve a data-analytics outcome.

dimensions.name: Prerequisite structure health
dimensions.justification: The graph is well-formed with readable, grade-conditioned prerequisites, but the analytics capstone-level courses sit deep: CS 482 Data Mining is at depth 7 (it requires CS 301, which itself chains back through the CS 113/114 sequence), and the deepestPrereqChain is 7. missingCourseCount is 2 (YWCC 207, YWCC 307), both peripheral seminars. For a minor aimed partly at non-CS students, reaching the most valuable analytics courses demands a long prerequisite ladder.
dimensions.suggestion: Offer at least one applied-analytics course with a short prerequisite path (e.g., statistics -> a single programming/data course) so non-CS minors can reach a genuine analytics capstone without completing the depth-7 CS 301 chain, and catalog YWCC 207/307.

dimensions.name: Learning outcomes & objectives
dimensions.justification: No program-level learning outcomes or objectives appear in the data; the minor is represented only by a referenced course pool. Prospective students cannot see what an analytics-minor graduate should be able to do (e.g., clean data, model, visualize, interpret).
dimensions.suggestion: Publish 4-5 learning outcomes for the minor covering data preparation, statistical modeling, mining/prediction, and communicating results with visualizations.

dimensions.name: Credit distribution & balance
dimensions.justification: There is no credit information to evaluate: statedTotalCredits is null, requirementGroups is 0, and creditReconcileDelta is null, so the required credit total and the required-versus-elective split are entirely absent. This is a credit-transparency gap rather than an internal inconsistency, but it leaves students unable to plan the minor.
dimensions.suggestion: Publish the minor's total credit requirement and how it divides between a required analytics core and electives (a typical 15-18 credit structure) directly on the page.

dimensions.name: Presentation & clarity
dimensions.justification: With requirementGroups=0 the page exposes no structure (no 'choose N from' lists, no core-versus-elective grouping) and no minor overview, and 2 course references dangle (YWCC 207/307). Course-level descriptions and prerequisites are present, but the minor as a unit is not legibly presented.
dimensions.suggestion: Add a short minor overview and an explicit requirement block listing the required courses and the elective menu so the analytics focus is visible at a glance.

dimensions.name: Industry & currency relevance
dimensions.justification: The classic analytics toolkit is represented (MATH 333/341 statistical inference, CS 482 data mining, IS 392 web mining, IS 333 social network analysis, database courses), which is solid but somewhat dated. Absent are the tools that now define the field: a Python/R data-science course, applied machine learning, data visualization/BI, and any data-engineering or big-data/cloud component.
dimensions.suggestion: Add an applied machine-learning course and a hands-on data-visualization/BI course (Python or R based) so the minor reflects how analytics is practiced today rather than stopping at classical statistics and mining.

descriptionQuality.justification: Descriptions are present and specific across the pool (e.g., CS 482 'covers the concepts and principles of advanced data mining systems design ... association and dependency analysis, classification, prediction, and clustering'; MATH 333 lists 'discrete and continuous distributions ... statistical inference for the mean and variance'). A few excerpts are shorter (IT 265 at ~149 characters), and all are truncated at ~220, but content is informative rather than placeholder.

prerequisiteClarity.justification: Prerequisites are unambiguous and grade-conditioned (e.g., 'CS 301 with a grade C or better' for CS 482, 'MATH 244 ... or MATH 333' for MATH 341), and only 2 peripheral references (YWCC 207/307) dangle. The depth is high but the statements themselves are clear and internally consistent.

missingTopics.rationale: The pool relies on CS 482 and stats courses but shows no dedicated data-programming course; modern analytics is built on Python/R data-wrangling, and without it the minor teaches concepts students cannot readily implement.

CS 482 Data Mining is the only predictive-modeling course and it sits at depth 7; a lighter applied-ML course (supervised/unsupervised methods, model evaluation) is now core to any analytics credential and is absent.
No visualization, dashboarding, or BI course appears, yet communicating findings visually (Tableau/Power BI/matplotlib) is the analytics skill employers most consistently demand.

missingTopics.topic: Data engineering and SQL at scale / ETL
missingTopics.rationale: CS 331 and IS 331 cover database design, but there is no ETL, data-warehousing, or big-data (Spark/cloud) content, leaving the pipeline that feeds analytics unaddressed.
For a data-analytics credential there is no course on responsible data use, bias, or privacy; given IS 333 harvests social-media data, an ethics/privacy component is a notable and topical gap.

emergingTrends.trend: Generative AI and LLMs for analytics
emergingTrends.rationale: LLM-assisted data analysis and natural-language querying are rapidly entering the analyst workflow; the minor should add exposure so graduates use current tooling rather than classical mining alone.
Analytics work is increasingly productionized; even at minor scope, awareness of versioned, reproducible pipelines would modernize the CS 482 / database thread.
Analytics now runs on Snowflake/BigQuery-style cloud warehouses; the database courses (CS 331/IS 331) could be extended toward cloud data platforms to match hiring demand.
Regulation and public scrutiny of data use are rising; a minor that already includes social-network and web mining (IS 333/IS 392) should position explicitly around ethical, privacy-aware analytics.
Batch statistics is giving way to streaming/real-time analytics in industry; positioning the mining and database courses toward streaming data would keep the minor current.

topSuggestions: Define an explicit required analytics core (statistics + databases + CS 482 data mining) and a curated elective list; the current referenced pool mixes analytics with unrelated art/design/media courses and shows no structure (requirementGroups=0).
Add a hands-on data-programming course (Python/R) and an applied machine-learning course so students can implement, not just study, analytics.
Add a data-visualization/BI course to cover the field's most-demanded communication skill, currently absent.
Publish the minor's total credits and required-versus-elective split (statedTotalCredits is null) and add a short overview so the minor is legible and plannable.
Provide a low-prerequisite route to a capstone analytics course so non-CS students can reach it without the depth-7 CS 301 chain.

design-of-the-user-experience-minor-264.name: Design of the User Experience Minor

dimensions.name: Curriculum coherence & progression
dimensions.justification: A genuine UX core is present and well-chosen: IS 247 Designing the User Experience, IS 375 Discovering User Needs for UX, IS 448 Usability & Measuring UX, and IE 355 Human Factors, supported by design foundations (AD 112, AD 150, DD 284/321, ID 203). However the referenced pool is diluted by a large block of game-programming and technical courses (IT 265/266/286/360/366/382/383/466/487, IT 230 security, IT 330 forensics, CS 482 data mining) that are not UX, and with requirementGroups=0 there is no required-core-then-elective progression shown.
dimensions.suggestion: Anchor the minor on a required UX core (IS 247 -> IS 375 -> IS 448 plus IE 355) and curate the elective list toward interaction and visual design, moving the game-programming and forensics/security courses out of the default UX menu.

dimensions.name: Prerequisite structure health
dimensions.justification: This program has the cleanest prerequisite data of the set: missingCourseCount is 0 with an empty missingCoursesSample, and prerequisites are readable and grade-conditioned. The deepestPrereqChain is 7, driven by advanced electives (IS 448 and IS 465 gated behind a statistics GUR, CS 482 behind CS 301), which is deep for a design-oriented audience even though the core UX courses (IS 247, IS 375 at depth 0) are accessible.
dimensions.suggestion: Keep the accessible UX core at the front and clearly flag the depth-7 electives (IS 448, IS 465, CS 482) as advanced/optional so design-focused students are not blocked by statistics or CS-algorithms prerequisites.

dimensions.name: Learning outcomes & objectives
dimensions.justification: No program-level learning outcomes or objectives are present in the data. A UX minor in particular benefits from stated outcomes (research, design, evaluation) because employers read UX competencies literally, and none are published here.
dimensions.suggestion: Publish 4-5 UX learning outcomes covering user research, interaction/visual design, prototyping, and usability evaluation.

dimensions.name: Credit distribution & balance
dimensions.justification: No credit information is available to assess balance: statedTotalCredits is null, requirementGroups is 0, and creditReconcileDelta is null. This is a credit-transparency gap for the minor rather than an internal inconsistency, but students cannot see the required total or how the UX core and electives divide.
dimensions.suggestion: Publish the minor's credit total and the split between the required UX core and electives (a typical 15-18 credit structure).

dimensions.name: Presentation & clarity
dimensions.justification: The underlying data is clean (missingCourseCount=0, no dangling references, clear course descriptions and prerequisites), which aids readability, but at the minor level there is no overview and no requirement structure (requirementGroups=0), so the page presents a course pool rather than a designed minor.
dimensions.suggestion: Add a brief UX-minor overview and an explicit requirement block (required core + 'choose N from' electives) so the strong UX spine is visible rather than buried in a long alphabetical pool.

dimensions.name: Industry & currency relevance
dimensions.justification: The UX content is current and marketable: IS 375 user-needs research, IS 448 usability and UX measurement, IE 355 human factors, and forward-looking interactive-media electives including IT 382/383 Game Design for XR and DD 321 Interactive and Reactive Environments. The visible gaps are the practitioner staples of accessibility, prototyping tools, and design systems rather than any dated emphasis.
dimensions.suggestion: Add an accessibility/inclusive-design course and a hands-on prototyping course (industry tools such as Figma) to cover the two competencies most requested of UX hires.

descriptionQuality.justification: Descriptions are specific and informative (e.g., IS 448 explains 'user experience research is the process of understanding why and how people use products and services ... usability refers to the ease of use and learnability'; IT 265 introduces 'core concepts and design methodologies integral to designing and developing games'). Excerpts are truncated at ~220 characters and a couple are shorter, but none read as placeholder text.

prerequisiteClarity.justification: Prerequisite clarity is the strongest in this set: missingCourseCount is 0 with no dangling references, and prerequisites are stated unambiguously with grade conditions (e.g., 'CS 113 or CS 115', 'IT 266 and either IT 114 or CS 114'). The graph is internally consistent end to end.

missingTopics.rationale: With IS 247/375/448 covering research and usability, the absence of any WCAG/accessibility course is the most conspicuous gap for a modern UX credential where accessibility is now a legal and hiring requirement.
The pool teaches research and evaluation but shows no course centered on hands-on interaction design and industry prototyping (Figma/Sketch), the concrete deliverable UX employers expect.
No IA or content-design course appears; structuring navigation, taxonomy, and content is a core UX competency distinct from the visual-design foundations (AD 112/150) present here.
Modern UX work is delivered through component-based design systems; the minor has visual foundations but nothing on systematized UI patterns bridging design and front-end.

IS 448 measures usability, but there is no dedicated A/B testing / UX analytics course, leaving the data-driven side of contemporary UX practice underweighted despite the available statistics courses.

emergingTrends.rationale: Generative UI and AI-assisted design are reshaping the discipline; a minor centered on IS 247/375/448 should introduce designing for and with AI to stay current.
IT 382/383 Game Design for XR and DD 321 already give the minor an XR foothold; extending these toward spatial/AR-VR UX positions graduates for a fast-growing interface frontier.
Voice and chat-based UX are now mainstream product surfaces not represented in the pool; adding conversational-design content would broaden the minor's relevance.
Regulation and platform policy are pushing accessibility and value-sensitive design to the center of UX; the minor should make inclusive, ethical design an explicit thread.
UX increasingly runs as an operational discipline embedded with engineering and product; exposure to design ops and handoff practice would make graduates immediately useful on teams.

topSuggestions: Anchor the minor on a required UX core (IS 247 -> IS 375 -> IS 448 plus IE 355) and curate electives toward design, moving the large game-programming and forensics/security block out of the default UX menu.
Add an accessibility/inclusive-design course and a hands-on prototyping course (Figma), the two most-requested UX competencies currently missing.
Publish the minor's credit total and required-versus-elective structure (statedTotalCredits is null; requirementGroups=0) plus a short UX overview.
Publish UX learning outcomes (research, design, prototyping, evaluation), none of which appear in the data.
Flag the depth-7 electives (IS 448, IS 465, CS 482) as advanced so design-focused students are not blocked by statistics/CS-algorithm prerequisites.

game-development-minor-265.name: Game Development Minor

dimensions.name: Curriculum coherence & progression
dimensions.justification: The minor is presented as an undifferentiated 40-course pool with no plan-of-study grid (requirementGroups=0), so the 15 credits carry no prescribed core-to-advanced progression; a student could satisfy it with five loosely related picks. The interdisciplinary breadth (AD 112/150, DD 284/321, COM 303/354, IE 355 Human Factors, plus IT 265/266/270/286/360/366/380) fits game development's cross-disciplinary nature, but the flagship advanced game courses (IT 466 3D Game Programming, IT 487 Advanced Game Production, IT 382/383 Game Design for XR) that appear in sibling YWCC minor pools are absent here.
dimensions.suggestion: Define a required spine (e.g., IT 265 Game Architecture -> IT 366 2D Game Programming -> IT 466 3D Game Programming) plus a curated art/UX elective set so the 15 credits form a deliberate progression rather than a menu.

dimensions.name: Prerequisite structure health
dimensions.justification: Prerequisite references are clean (missingCourseCount=0, no dangling codes), a genuine strength, but the deepest chain is 7 and several pool courses sit very deep: IS 333 Social Network Analysis, IS 448 Usability, and IS 465 are at depth 7, and IS 421 at depth 5. In a 15-credit minor those courses are effectively unreachable without the parent IS/IT major sequence, so a meaningful part of the advertised pool is inaccessible to typical minor students.
dimensions.suggestion: Mark which pool courses are realistically reachable inside a 15-credit minor and publish a low-prerequisite entry path (e.g., IT 265/266/270/286 at depths 1-2) so students are not steered toward depth-7 courses they cannot enroll in.

dimensions.name: Learning outcomes & objectives
dimensions.justification: No program-level learning outcomes or educational objectives appear anywhere in the provided data; only a course pool with per-course descriptions is present. For a catalog page this absence is a real finding, and it is especially costly for a skills-based credential like game development where employers look for demonstrable competencies.
dimensions.suggestion: Publish 4-6 explicit minor outcomes (e.g., 'build and ship a playable 2D/3D game', 'apply game AI and real-time graphics techniques', 'conduct playtesting/UX evaluation') so the minor's value is legible.

dimensions.name: Credit distribution & balance
dimensions.justification: As a minor there is no plan-of-study grid and summedTermCredits/creditReconcileDelta are null, so this is a credit-transparency point rather than a reconciliation defect: statedTotalCredits=15 (five 3-credit courses) is stated but the page gives no split between a required core and electives. Every pool course is a uniform 3 credits, which keeps arithmetic simple but hides how much of the 15 is prescribed versus free choice.
dimensions.suggestion: State the 15-credit structure explicitly (e.g., 9 credits required game core + 6 credits curated electives) so students can see the required-versus-elective balance at a glance.

dimensions.name: Presentation & clarity
dimensions.justification: No plan grid is expected for a minor, and course descriptions are legible and substantive. Clarity is undercut, however, by a pool that omits the very advanced game courses (IT 466, IT 487, IT 382/383) present in sibling minors while mixing in many non-game courses (HIST 387, ID 203, IE 355) with no required/optional labeling, so the reader cannot tell what the minor actually centers on.
dimensions.suggestion: Present a clearly labeled required-core / elective structure and include the advanced game-production and XR courses so the page reflects the minor's stated focus.

dimensions.name: Industry & currency relevance
dimensions.justification: Game-specific coverage is respectable (IT 265 Game Architecture, IT 366 2D Game Programming, IT 270 3D Modeling, IT 286 Foundations of Game Production, IT 360 Computer Graphics for VFX) and UX is well served (IS 247/375/448). But there is no dedicated modern game-engine course (Unity/Unreal, C#/C++ pipelines), no game AI (pathfinding, behavior trees, PCG), no multiplayer/networking, and no game audio, and the XR courses are absent from this pool despite existing in the catalog.
dimensions.suggestion: Add or modernize an engine-centric build course and a game-AI course, and surface the existing XR (IT 382/383) offerings so the minor tracks current studio practice.

descriptionQuality.justification: Sampled descriptions are present and specific across the pool (e.g., DD 284 Video and Animation, IS 322 Mobile Applications, IT 366 2D Game Programming), with no thin/placeholder excerpts observed; restriction notes are included where relevant.

prerequisiteClarity.justification: Prerequisites are stated unambiguously with grade conditions (e.g., COM 303/354 require 'ENGL 102 with a grade of C or higher'), and missingCourseCount=0 means no dangling references; depth-0 intro courses correctly carry none.

missingTopics.rationale: The pool leans on IT 366 2D Game Programming and IT 270 3D Modeling but has no course anchored in a contemporary engine with scripting (C#/C++), scene/ECS architecture, and asset pipelines; the catalog's IT 466 3D Game Programming should be surfaced and modernized as engine-based.

missingTopics.topic: Gameplay AI and procedural content generation
missingTopics.rationale: No course covers pathfinding, finite-state machines, behavior trees, or PCG, which are core to shipping games; a dedicated Game AI course would fill the largest technical gap in the pool.

IT 120 (intro networking) and IT 490 (systems integration) are generic; there is no netcode, client-server, or real-time synchronization content despite multiplayer being standard in commercial titles.
The pool covers art, animation, and graphics (AD/DD/IT 360) but has no audio component, leaving a full production discipline unrepresented.
IT 286 Foundations of Game Production is present but the advanced production course (IT 487) is missing from the pool, so there is no shipped-game capstone to anchor a student portfolio.

emergingTrends.rationale: The catalog already offers IT 382/383 Game Design for XR but they are absent from this minor's pool; surfacing them would position students for the fastest-growing interactive platform.
Procedural asset generation, LLM-driven NPC dialogue, and AI-assisted art are reshaping production pipelines, yet nothing in the pool touches AI-assisted content creation.

emergingTrends.trend: Real-time 3D beyond entertainment (virtual production, serious games, digital twins)
emergingTrends.rationale: IT 360 Computer Graphics for VFX and IT 380 Educational Software Design hint at adjacent uses; game-engine skills increasingly transfer to film, training, and simulation markets.

emergingTrends.trend: Data-driven / live-service design and player analytics
emergingTrends.rationale: IS 333 Social Network Analysis and IS 392 Web Mining exist in the parent pool; games-as-a-service depend on telemetry and analytics that this minor could leverage.
IS 322 Mobile Applications is in scope; mobile remains the largest games platform, so a mobile game-build path would sharpen employability.

topSuggestions: Convert the flat 40-course pool into a required game core plus curated electives with an explicit progression (architecture -> 2D -> 3D -> production capstone).
Add the missing modern competencies: a game-engine build course, a game-AI course, and game audio; surface the existing XR courses (IT 382/383) that are absent from the pool.
Publish program-level learning outcomes tied to demonstrable deliverables (ship a playable game, build a portfolio).
Restore the advanced game courses (IT 466 3D Game Programming, IT 487 Advanced Game Production) to the displayed pool so the minor reflects its own focus.
State the 15-credit required-vs-elective split and flag which deep-prerequisite pool courses are realistically reachable within the minor.

information-technology-minor-for-ywcc-ma.name: Information Technology Minor (for YWCC majors)

dimensions.name: Curriculum coherence & progression
dimensions.justification: The pool does contain a legitimate IT infrastructure spine (IT 120 Network Technology -> IT 202 Internet Applications -> IT 340 System Administration / IT 420 Computer Systems and Networks -> IT 490 Systems Integration, plus IS 331 Databases), which gives the minor real coherence potential. That spine is diluted, however, by nine game-design courses (IT 265/266/270/286/366/382/383/466/487) in a 32-course pool with no plan-of-study grid, so the IT identity is buried in a menu.
dimensions.suggestion: Define an explicit IT core progression (networking -> systems administration -> systems integration, plus databases and web) and move the game courses out of the required IT set.

dimensions.name: Prerequisite structure health
dimensions.justification: References are clean (missingCourseCount=0, no dangling codes), a real strength. The deepest chain is 7 with IS 465 at depth 7 and IT 340/420/IS 421 at depth 5; since this minor is for YWCC (computing) majors, those students generally do hold the underlying prerequisites, so accessibility is less of a problem here than for the non-YWCC version.
dimensions.suggestion: Publish a recommended prerequisite-aware ordering so YWCC majors slot the IT courses efficiently around their major sequence.

dimensions.name: Learning outcomes & objectives
dimensions.justification: No program-level learning outcomes or objectives appear in the data; only course descriptions are present. For a catalog credential this is a real finding, and it also makes it impossible to see how this minor complements a student's YWCC major.
dimensions.suggestion: Publish outcomes describing the IT breadth a YWCC major gains (e.g., 'administer networked systems', 'integrate enterprise IT components', 'manage relational data stores').

dimensions.name: Credit distribution & balance
dimensions.justification: As a minor there is no term grid and summedTermCredits/creditReconcileDelta are null, so this is a transparency point rather than a defect: statedTotalCredits=15 is stated but with no required-versus-elective breakdown. All courses are uniform 3 credits.
dimensions.suggestion: State the 15-credit structure and, importantly, list which of the student's own YWCC-major courses may not be double-counted toward the minor.

dimensions.name: Presentation & clarity

The entire policy rationale for a separate 'for YWCC majors' entry (different eligible/countable courses) is invisible in the presented data, which is a genuine clarity defect.
dimensions.suggestion: Differentiate this entry from the non-YWCC version by showing the eligibility rules and the course set that YWCC majors may actually count.

dimensions.name: Industry & currency relevance
dimensions.justification: The IT infrastructure coverage is solid and job-relevant (networking IT 120/420, system administration IT 340, systems integration IT 490, databases IS 331, web/internet IT 202/IS 218), but the pool has no cybersecurity and no cloud/virtualization content, which are the two defining competencies of contemporary IT practice and are expected under ACM IT2017 guidance.
dimensions.suggestion: Add an information-security course and a cloud/virtualization course to the IT core.

descriptionQuality.justification: Course descriptions are present and specific across the pool (IT 120 Network Technology, IT 340 System Administration, IS 331 Database Design), with no thin or placeholder excerpts observed.

prerequisiteClarity.justification: Prerequisites are stated with clear course logic and grade conditions where relevant, and missingCourseCount=0 confirms no dangling references across the 32-course pool.

missingTopics.rationale: A 32-course IT pool with networking and system administration but no security course is a serious gap; ACM IT2017 treats information assurance and security as a pervasive pillar, so a Network/Information Security course is the highest-value addition.

IT 340 System Administration and IT 420 Computer Systems and Networks are traditional on-premises courses; there is no AWS/Azure/GCP, containers, or infrastructure-as-code, which is where IT operations now run.
IT 490 Systems Integration is the closest fit, but there is no dedicated scripting-for-operations, configuration-management, or CI/CD content that modern IT roles require.
IS 331 Databases is present but there is no data-warehouse, business-intelligence, or analytics course, even though IT increasingly centers on data pipelines and reporting.

missingTopics.topic: A curated IT identity over game bloat
missingTopics.rationale: Nine game-design courses dominate the pool while security and cloud are absent, so the pool does not read as a coherent contemporary IT skill set.

emergingTrends.rationale: IT is shifting to cloud, containers, and declarative provisioning; IT 340/420 should gain cloud modules so YWCC majors leave with current operations skills.
An escalating threat landscape makes security literacy mandatory for every IT professional, and this pool currently offers none.
AI-driven monitoring, incident triage, and copilots are reshaping IT operations and support, an area no current course touches.
IT 120/420 networking should extend to IoT device management and edge architectures that are expanding the IT footprint.
Regulatory pressure (GDPR/CCPA) makes governance and compliance a core IT competency that complements the minor's data and systems courses.

topSuggestions: Add cybersecurity and cloud/virtualization courses, the two most conspicuous gaps in a modern IT pool.
Differentiate this minor from the identical-looking 'not for YWCC majors' and 'Mobile and Web' pools by showing the eligibility rules and countable course set specific to YWCC majors.
Define a coherent IT core progression and separate the nine game courses that currently dilute the pool.
Publish program-level learning outcomes describing the IT breadth a YWCC major gains.
State the 15-credit required-vs-elective structure, including which courses a student cannot double-count from their YWCC major.

information-technology-minor-not-for-ywc.name: Information Technology Minor (not for YWCC majors

dimensions.name: Curriculum coherence & progression
dimensions.justification: The pool carries a real IT infrastructure spine (IT 120 -> IT 202 -> IT 340/IT 420 -> IT 490, plus IS 331 Databases), but as with the sibling IT minor it is diluted by nine game-design courses in a 32-course pool with no plan-of-study grid. For non-computing students this incoherence is more damaging, because they have no major context to help them assemble a sensible five-course path from the menu.
dimensions.suggestion: For a non-YWCC audience, prescribe a fixed IT core sequence rather than a pool so students without a computing background follow a coherent introduction-to-IT path.

dimensions.name: Prerequisite structure health
dimensions.justification: References are clean (missingCourseCount=0), but the prerequisite depth is the central problem for THIS audience: the deepest chain is 7 (IS 465 depth 7; IT 340/420 and IS 421 at depth 5) and those chains run back through CS/IS courses that non-YWCC students will not have taken. A large share of the advertised pool is therefore effectively unreachable for the exact students this minor targets.
dimensions.suggestion: Curate a low-prerequisite pathway (IT 120, IS 117, IT 202 at depths 0-2 with clearly stated non-computing entry points) and drop or gate the depth-5-to-7 courses that non-majors cannot realistically enter.

dimensions.name: Learning outcomes & objectives
dimensions.justification: No program-level learning outcomes or objectives are present; only course descriptions exist. This is a real finding for any catalog page and is especially important here, since non-computing students rely on stated outcomes to judge whether the minor is appropriate for them.
dimensions.suggestion: Publish outcomes aimed at non-computing students (e.g., 'apply IT tools to a home discipline', 'administer basic networked systems', 'manage and query data').

dimensions.name: Credit distribution & balance
dimensions.justification: As a minor there is no term grid and summedTermCredits/creditReconcileDelta are null, so this is a transparency point: statedTotalCredits=15 is given without a required-versus-elective breakdown. Uniform 3-credit courses keep arithmetic simple.
dimensions.suggestion: State the 15-credit structure and clearly mark any prerequisite courses a non-major must take first, since those could push the real cost above 15 credits.

dimensions.name: Presentation & clarity
dimensions.justification: The displayed course pool is byte-identical to the 'for YWCC majors' IT minor and to the Mobile and Web minor, so the catalog provides no way to see what makes this non-major credential different, and the parsed name is even truncated ('...not for YWCC majors' with no closing parenthesis). Both issues are genuine presentation defects.
dimensions.suggestion: Fix the truncated program name and present a distinct, non-major-appropriate course list rather than reusing the shared pool.

dimensions.name: Industry & currency relevance
dimensions.justification: The IT infrastructure content (networking IT 120/420, system administration IT 340, systems integration IT 490, databases IS 331, web IT 202/IS 218) is relevant, but for a non-major audience the more current draw would be applied IT literacy, and the pool lacks cybersecurity and cloud entirely while over-indexing on game courses.
dimensions.suggestion: Add an accessible security-and-privacy course and a cloud-literacy course pitched at non-computing students.

descriptionQuality.justification: Descriptions are present and specific throughout the pool (IT 120 Network Technology, IT 202 Internet Applications, IS 331 Database Design), with no thin or placeholder text observed.

prerequisiteClarity.justification: Prerequisite statements themselves are clear and non-dangling (missingCourseCount=0), but for a non-computing audience the clarity gap is that many prerequisites presuppose CS/IS courses these students lack, and the page does not flag which courses are open to non-majors.

missingTopics.rationale: There is no security course in a pool that includes networking and system administration; security is the highest-value addition and is especially relevant framing for non-computing students.

missingTopics.topic: Cloud computing and IT service literacy
missingTopics.rationale: IT 340/IT 420 are traditional on-premises courses with no cloud, virtualization, or SaaS content, yet cloud services are how most non-computing professionals actually encounter IT.

missingTopics.topic: Applied data management and analytics for non-majors
missingTopics.rationale: IS 331 Databases is technical; a non-major audience would benefit from an applied data/analytics course connecting IT to their home discipline.
The pool is dominated by depth-4-to-7 IS/IT courses and nine game courses, but offers little that a student with no computing prerequisites can start with, so the pool is mismatched to its audience.
No course teaches basic scripting/automation, which is arguably the most transferable IT skill for students from other majors.

emergingTrends.trend: Cloud and SaaS-based IT for non-specialists
emergingTrends.rationale: Non-computing professionals increasingly manage cloud tools; the minor should teach cloud literacy rather than only on-premises system administration.

emergingTrends.trend: Cybersecurity awareness and privacy for all disciplines
emergingTrends.rationale: Every field now faces security and privacy obligations, making a non-major security course both timely and broadly marketable.
AI copilots are transforming how non-technical professionals use IT; an AI-literacy component would strongly differentiate this non-major minor.
Non-computing students can deliver real value through no-code platforms, a fast-growing area the current IS/IT-heavy pool does not address.

emergingTrends.trend: Data governance and compliance across professions
emergingTrends.rationale: GDPR/CCPA-style obligations reach every discipline, so governance literacy would make this minor relevant to non-computing majors' careers.

topSuggestions: Replace the shared 32-course pool with a curated low-prerequisite IT pathway that non-computing students can actually complete without unstated CS/IS prerequisites.
Fix the truncated program name and differentiate this entry from the identical 'for YWCC majors' and 'Mobile and Web' course lists.
Add accessible cybersecurity and cloud-literacy courses aimed at non-majors, the competencies most missing and most marketable.
Publish learning outcomes framed for non-computing students so they can judge fit.
State the 15-credit structure and flag prerequisite courses that could raise the true credit cost for a non-major.

mobile-and-web-minor-266.name: Mobile and Web Minor

dimensions.name: Curriculum coherence & progression
dimensions.justification: The 32-course pool is not curated to 'mobile and web': it is the full IS/IT inventory and includes nine game courses (IT 265/266/270/286/366/382/383/466/487) and infrastructure courses (IT 340 System Administration, IT 420 Computer Systems and Networks, IT 490 Systems Integration) that have no clear mobile/web rationale. With no plan-of-study grid (requirementGroups=0), the minor reads as an unfiltered menu rather than a coherent web+mobile progression.
dimensions.suggestion: Replace the generic pool with a curated sequence (IS 117 -> IS 218 -> IS 219/IS 421 for web, IS 322 for mobile, IS 331 for data) and remove the unrelated game and sysadmin courses.

dimensions.name: Prerequisite structure health
dimensions.justification: References are clean (missingCourseCount=0) with no dangling codes, but the deepest chain is 7 and marquee courses sit deep (IS 465 at depth 7, IS 421/IT 340/IT 420 at depth 5, IS 322/IS 390 at depth 4). For a 15-credit minor many of these are unreachable without the underlying IS/IT major sequence, so the advertised pool overstates what a minor student can actually take.
dimensions.suggestion: Provide a reachable low-prerequisite path (IS 117 and IT 202 at depth 0-2) and label which advanced courses require major-level prerequisites.

dimensions.name: Learning outcomes & objectives
dimensions.justification: No program-level learning outcomes or objectives are present in the data; only course descriptions exist. For a skills-oriented web/mobile credential this omission is a real finding because employers evaluate this minor on concrete build competencies.
dimensions.suggestion: Publish outcomes such as 'design and deploy a responsive web application', 'build and ship a mobile app', and 'model and query an application database'.

dimensions.name: Credit distribution & balance
dimensions.justification: As a minor there is no term grid and summedTermCredits/creditReconcileDelta are null, so this is a transparency point, not a reconciliation defect: statedTotalCredits=15 is given but with no required-versus-elective breakdown. Uniform 3-credit courses keep the arithmetic clean but the page does not show how much of the 15 is prescribed.
dimensions.suggestion: State the 15-credit structure (required web+mobile core versus electives) so students see the balance explicitly.

dimensions.name: Presentation & clarity
dimensions.justification: The most visible clarity problem is that the displayed course pool is byte-identical to the two Information Technology minors and contradicts this minor's own title, listing game-design and system-administration courses under 'Mobile and Web'. A reader cannot tell what the minor is about from its course list, which is a genuine presentation defect even though individual descriptions are legible.
dimensions.suggestion: Align the displayed course list with the minor's title so game/sysadmin courses are removed and web/mobile courses are foregrounded.

dimensions.name: Industry & currency relevance
dimensions.justification: The genuinely relevant courses (IS 117/218/219 website development, IS 421 Advanced Web Applications, IS 322 Mobile Applications, IS 331 Databases, IS 373 CMS, IT 202 Internet Applications) cover real ground but skew toward traditional website development: there is no modern JavaScript framework (React/Vue), no explicit REST/GraphQL API design, no web/mobile security, and no cloud deployment or cross-platform mobile (React Native/Flutter).
dimensions.suggestion: Modernize the core toward component-based front-end frameworks, API-first backends, mobile cross-platform frameworks, and cloud deployment/security.

descriptionQuality.justification: Descriptions are present and informative across sampled courses (IS 218 Building Web Applications, IS 219 Advanced Website Development, IS 322 Mobile Applications), with no thin or placeholder text observed.

prerequisiteClarity.justification: Prerequisites are stated explicitly and consistently (e.g., IS 218/219 require '(IS 117 or IT 202) and (CS 100, CS 113, or CS 115)'), and missingCourseCount=0 confirms no dangling references.

missingTopics.rationale: The pool's web courses (IS 117/218/219) are website-development oriented with no explicit React/Vue/Angular, component architecture, or single-page-application content that defines current front-end work.
No course in the pool addresses authentication, OWASP risks, secure APIs, or HTTPS/TLS, despite security being non-negotiable for anything deployed to the web or an app store.

missingTopics.topic: Cloud deployment and DevOps for web
missingTopics.rationale: IT 340 (sysadmin) and IT 420 (networks) are generic infrastructure; there is no cloud-native deployment, containers, CI/CD, serverless, or CDN content that modern web/mobile delivery relies on.
API-first design is implied inside IS 218/421 but never taught explicitly, yet decoupled front-ends and mobile clients depend on well-designed service APIs.

missingTopics.topic: Cross-platform mobile and Progressive Web Apps
missingTopics.rationale: IS 322 is the only mobile course; there is no React Native/Flutter or PWA content, which is exactly the bridge between the 'mobile' and 'web' halves of this minor.

emergingTrends.rationale: Nothing in the pool positions students for integrating LLM APIs, chat UIs, or AI copilots, now a baseline expectation for web/mobile products and for developer productivity.

emergingTrends.trend: Progressive Web Apps, WebAssembly, and edge computing
emergingTrends.rationale: The web platform is moving toward near-native performance and edge delivery; the minor's website-centric courses should incorporate PWA and edge patterns.
IS 373 teaches traditional CMS; the field has shifted to headless CMS, static generation, and serverless functions that this minor could adopt.
WCAG accessibility and GDPR/CCPA privacy are legal and market requirements for shipped web/mobile apps, yet no course covers them.

emergingTrends.trend: Mobile-first, API-first convergence of web and mobile
emergingTrends.rationale: Shared component and API layers increasingly unify web and mobile front-ends, which is the natural organizing theme this minor should teach explicitly.

topSuggestions: Curate the course list to actually match the minor's title, removing the nine game courses and the sysadmin/networks courses that are inherited from a shared pool.
Modernize the web/mobile core toward JavaScript frameworks (React), REST/GraphQL APIs, and cross-platform mobile so it reflects current practice rather than legacy website development.
Add web/mobile security and cloud deployment, the two competencies most conspicuously absent from the pool.
Publish concrete build-oriented learning outcomes for the minor.
State the 15-credit required-vs-elective structure and flag which advanced courses need major-level prerequisites.
